commit 5abbf5c6041cf3d7d074365a249964e932a170ce
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/hugetlb: fix memory offline failure due to hwpoisoned file hugetlb

When a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, me_huge_page() keeps the folio in
pagecache with an increased refcount and PG_hwpoison set. This commit aims
to fix memory offline failures by temporarily marking the folio and dropping
the memory-failure refcount when deleting it from the pagecache.

> diff --git a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> index 3f70c47981de..6bebe2e67f3e 100644
> --- a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> +++ b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> @@ -603,6 +603,11 @@ static void remove_inode_hugepages(struct inode *inode, loff_t lstart,
>  							index, truncate_op))
>  				freed++;
>  
> +			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
> +				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
> +				folio_put(folio);
> +			}
> +

Can this race with memory_failure() and cause a double free of the folio?

If try_memory_failure_hugetlb() increments the folio refcount and sets
PG_hwpoison before acquiring folio_lock():

CPU1 (memory_failure):
    __get_huge_page_for_hwpoison() // increments refcount, sets PG_hwpoison
    // blocks on folio_lock()

CPU2 (remove_inode_hugepages):
    folio_lock(folio);
    hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache(folio); // sets folio->mapping = NULL
    folio_unlock(folio);
    if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
        folio_put(folio); // drops the refcount CPU1 just acquired
    }

Then CPU1 acquires the lock, reaches me_huge_page(), sees
!folio_mapping(folio), and unconditionally calls folio_put() again,
dropping the same reference a second time.

Can this also cause a refcount underflow if PG_hwpoison is set without an
extra reference being held?

If a hardware memory error occurs on a hugetlb folio that is temporarily
isolated (like during migration), __get_huge_page_for_hwpoison() can return
MF_HUGETLB_RETRY. On its second pass with MF_NO_RETRY set, it calls
hugetlb_update_hwpoison() to set the PG_hwpoison flag but skips incrementing
the refcount. When the folio is later processed here, it will see PG_hwpoison
set and call folio_put(folio), dropping a non-existent reference.

Does bypassing dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio() here corrupt hugetlb pool
accounting?

When folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio) is called, free_huge_folio() will
eventually check this flag and call remove_hugetlb_folio(h, folio, false),
which hardcodes adjust_surplus to false. If the hwpoisoned folio was
originally a surplus page, surplus_huge_pages is never decremented.

Additionally, the normal hardware poison dissolution path in
dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio() explicitly executes h->max_huge_pages-- to
reflect the permanent loss of the page. By marking it temporary and
bypassing the dissolution function, max_huge_pages remains unmodified,
skewing the capacity statistics.

>  			mutex_unlock(&hugetlb_fault_mutex_table[hash]);
>  		}

commit 5abbf5c6041cf3d7d074365a249964e932a170ce
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>
Date:   Wed Mar 18 02:07:11 2026 +0000

    mm/hugetlb: fix memory offline failure due to hwpoisoned file hugetlb
    
    When a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, me_huge_page() will keep the
    hugetlb folio in pagcahe with refcount increased and PG_hwpoison set. Even
    after the hugetlb file is deleted, the hugetlb folio is still leaked.
    
    If we want to offline the memory block that the hwpoisoned hugetlb folio
    belongs to, it fails in dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios() due to the
    hwpoisoned hugetlb folio isn't free.
    
    I can reproduce this issue with the following steps in qemu:
     1) echo offline >/sys/devices/system/memory/auto_online_blocks
     2) in qemu monitor:
           object_add memory-backend-ram,id=mem10,size=1G
           device_add pc-dimm,id=dimm1,memdev=mem10,node=2
     3) echo online_movable > /sys/devices/system/node/node2/memory136/state
     4) echo 5 > /sys/devices/system/node/node2/hugepages/hugepages-2048kB/nr_hugepages
     5) run ./hugetlb_file. This process will receive SIGBUS.
     6) remove the hugetlbfs file.
     7) echo offline > /sys/devices/system/node/node2/memory136/state
    
    hugetlb_file.c:
      fd = open("/dev/hugepages/my_hugepage_file", O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0755);
      fallocate(fd, 0, 0, HUGEPAGE_SIZE * 2);
      addr = mmap(NULL, HUGEPAGE_SIZE * 2, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,
                    MAP_SHARED | MAP_HUGETLB, fd, 0);
      memset(addr, 0xaa, HUGEPAGE_SIZE * 2);
      madvise(addr, HUGEPAGE_SIZE, MADV_HWPOISON);
    
    To fix it, when deleting hugetlb folio from pagecache, mark the hugetlb
    folio temporary, and put the refcount increased by memory-failure. After
    the hugetlb folio is deleted from pagecache, the refcount is decreased to
    zero and the hugetlb folio is dissolved.
    
    Signed-off-by: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

diff --git a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
index 3f70c47981de..6bebe2e67f3e 100644
--- a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
+++ b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
@@ -603,6 +603,11 @@ static void remove_inode_hugepages(struct inode *inode, loff_t lstart,
 							index, truncate_op))
 				freed++;
 
+			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
+				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
+				folio_put(folio);
+			}
+
 			mutex_unlock(&hugetlb_fault_mutex_table[hash]);
 		}
 		folio_batch_release(&fbatch);

Okay, let's break this down. `hwpoison_user_mappings` is a simple boolean; it doesn't do any refcount management. So, if `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` calls it and it *fails*, that means we return an `MF_FAILED` action and the folio is unlocked, but the crucial point is that the *refcount isn't decremented*. That's a leak! We've pinned the memory for a failure that didn't happen, or didn't complete.

This implies that the `MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED` flag leaves an extra refcount on the folio regardless of success or failure during the unmapping process.

But wait, if the memory failure *succeeds* in unmapping, `identify_page_state` is called. For anonymous HugeTLB pages, `me_huge_page` *does* call `folio_put(folio)`. However, for *file* HugeTLB pages, it sets `extra_pins = true` and *skips* the `folio_put` call. This means that for file HugeTLB pages, the memory failure pin is *always* leaked! This is a significant issue.

If the memory failure pin leaks on a file huge page, how is it ever cleaned up? The commit message sheds some light on this. It says that when a file HugeTLB folio encounters an Uncorrectable Error (UCE), `me_huge_page()` keeps the folio in the page cache with an incremented refcount and `PG_hwpoison` set. The folio is leaked, even after the HugeTLB file is deleted.

The fix, according to the author, is to explicitly drop the refcount in `remove_inode_hugepages`:

    "thought": "**Analysis of Hugetlbfs and Memory Failure Interaction**

Okay, here's my current understanding, distilled from this deep dive: the crucial interaction point is how `memory_failure` and `remove_inode_hugepages` work together on hugetlb folios, particularly file-backed ones. I've uncovered a potential double-put vulnerability that needs immediate attention.

My main takeaway is that `hugetlbfs` does *not* utilize `truncate_inode_pages`, but relies instead on `remove_inode_hugepages` for folio removal from the page cache. The core function is `remove_inode_single_folio`. Given the locking in place, it seems like concurrent removals *should* be impossible, but...

The race I've pinpointed occurs between `remove_inode_hugepages` and `memory_failure`. `memory_failure` can cause an "extra" reference count on a folio if the folio is hwpoisoned. `remove_inode_hugepages`, upon seeing `PG_hwpoison` set, assumes this extra count and calls `folio_put`. However, if `remove_inode_hugepages` runs concurrently with `memory_failure`, the timing gets very tricky:

1.  `remove_inode_hugepages` grabs the folio, calls `remove_inode_single_folio` (which removes the folio from the page cache), and *then* checks `PG_hwpoison`.
2.  Concurrently, `memory_failure` targets the *same* folio. It elevates the reference count and sets `PG_hwpoison`.
3.  `remove_inode_hugepages` sees `PG_hwpoison`, thinks the refcount was leaked, and drops it.
4.  `memory_failure` continues, and then, finding `mapping == NULL`, drops the reference count.

This leads to a double-put scenario, with potential for a reference count underflow and UAF! It looks like the original code introduces the race, and the author's patch exacerbates this with the added `folio_put(folio)` call in `remove_inode_hugepages` when `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` returns true.

The fix I'm considering is to synchronize the refcount drops inside `memory_failure`. The heart of the problem is that `remove_inode_hugepages` assumes any `PG_hwpoison` page needs a put, and does not check to see if the `memory_failure` call is active. The state of "memory failure has elevated the refcount" is not tracked. I think the only way to avoid this is to synchronize.

The concern with `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` is likely not a concern, given how hwpoisoned folios behave, that is, they should not be re-allocated. I'm also now thinking that `PG_hwpoison` can *only* be set by memory failure, so the only path to the double put is the one I have identified.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Hugetlbfs Error Handling in Truncation**

Okay, so I'm looking at how hugetlbfs handles errors during file truncation, specifically when dealing with memory failures. The `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` function immediately jumps out at me because, well, it's defined and *literally* returns 0 and does nothing.

My initial thought was, "Okay, that's not very helpful, but maybe it's used somewhere else." I dig a bit deeper and see it's used in `truncate_error_folio()` and my concern is that this is how things work. Inside `fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c`, the function is defined:

```c
static int hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio(struct address_space *mapping,
				struct folio *folio)
{
	return 0;
}
```

This *does* get called by `truncate_error_folio()`, which then calls `filemap_release_folio()`. Now, `filemap_release_folio()` usually deals with buffer management. Hugetlbfs pages generally don't *have* buffers, so `filemap_release_folio()` probably just returns 1 in this context. But wait, does it actually remove the folio from the page cache? No, it just releases buffers if they exist! `filemap_release_folio` doesn't remove the folio.

So, *effectively*, `truncate_error_folio()` calls my no-op `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio`, then a no-op buffer release, and *never* actually removes the folio from the page cache! I can see this right in the code block for `truncate_error_folio`:

```c
	if (mapping->a_ops->error_remove_folio) {
        ... // calls it, and checks filemap_release_folio. 
        // IT NEVER REMOVES THE FOLIO FROM PAGE CACHE!
```

Wait, then `generic_error_remove_folio` *does* the right thing and calls `truncate_inode_folio(mapping, folio)` which *does* remove the folio!

But wait... how are these "error" folios handled after a memory failure? Because `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` leaves the folio in page cache, but something else must have been incrementing the refcount via `page_handle_poison` / `get_hwpoison_page`.

I see that `me_huge_page()` has this comment: `/* The page is kept in page cache. */ extra_pins = true;` And now I see *why*! The folio *is* kept in the page cache! I see this in `me_huge_page()`:

```c
	mapping = folio_mapping(folio);
	if (mapping) {
		res = truncate_error_folio(folio, page_to_pfn(p), mapping);
		/* The page is kept in page cache. */
		extra_pins = true;
		folio_unlock(folio);
	} else {
...
```

So `truncate_error_folio()` leaves the folio in the page cache, and `me_huge_page()` seems to know about it. It marks extra pins because the folio is staying put.

The more pressing question is: who decrements this mysterious refcount that was taken from `get_hwpoison_page`? Where does the refcount go?

Alright, let's break this down. My initial concern is around how refcounts are managed when dealing with hardware poisoned folios, specifically in the context of hugetlb pages.     "thought": "**Hugetlb Folio Reference Accounting During Memory Failure**

Okay, let's break this down. It seems the core of the issue revolves around how hugetlb folios are handled when a memory failure occurs, specifically the reference counting and potential leaks.

First, `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` calls `hugetlb_update_hwpoison`, which, if I'm understanding this correctly, sets the `PG_hwpoison` bit. It also uses `folio_try_get()` if the page is migratable, which seems right, and returns `MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED`. In `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`, after `get_huge_page_for_hwpoison`, a lock is taken on the folio. Then, `hwpoison_user_mappings` is called, and `identify_page_state` gets involved, which will call `me_huge_page()` - this is where things get interesting.

`me_huge_page` receives the folio and calls `truncate_error_folio`. So, if it's a file-backed hugetlb folio, `memory_failure` *does* keep an extra reference, because it's in the page cache (`mapping != NULL`).

Now, if a hugetlbfs file is experiencing a memory failure, a reference is taken, `PG_hwpoison` is set, and `truncate_error_folio` is called. `truncate_error_folio` tries to call `mapping->a_ops->error_remove_folio`, which for hugetlbfs is `hugetlbs_error_remove_folio`. But that function returns 0 and does nothing. Then, it returns `MF_RECOVERED`, but crucially, it doesn't unmap or remove the folio from the page cache. Though, `hwpoison_user_mappings()` called by `me_huge_page` does call `try_to_unmap()`, so the folio is unmapped from the user process but stays in the pagecache. The extra reference is retained.

This extra reference persists. Later, when the file is deleted (e.g., `remove_inode_hugepages`), folios are iterated. `remove_inode_single_folio` removes them from the page cache. And *here's* the potential problem. If `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` is true, the current patch does:

```c
			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
				folio_put(folio);
			}
```

Okay, *but* what if that hwpoisoned folio came from a *different* process experiencing a memory failure, or *worse*, what if `memory_failure` failed *before* it could take the reference? The problem becomes the *atomicity* of the operations. The `folio_try_get()` call in `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` happens *before* `TestSetPageHWPoison(page)`. So, if `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` is true, is there a *guarantee* that `memory_failure` actually took a reference, or that it successfully dropped it?

What about `soft_offline_page`? It calls `get_hwpoison_page()`, but without `MF_COUNT_INCREASED`. What does `soft_offline_page` do for hugetlb pages? Ah, it calls `get_any_page()`. Does that *guarantee* a drop, even if it fails? What if `memory_failure` itself fails midway, say if `hwpoison_filter` or `hwpoison_user_mappings` fail? Does the code properly handle that?

Let's look at `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` again:

```c
	folio = page_folio(p);
	folio_lock(folio);

	if (hwpoison_filter(p)) {
		folio_clear_hugetlb_hwpoison(folio);
		folio_unlock(folio);
		if (res == MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED)
			folio_put(folio);
		return -EOPNOTSUPP;
	}

...
	if (res == MF_HUGETLB_FREED) { ... }

	if (!hwpoison_user_mappings(folio, p, pfn, flags)) {
		folio_unlock(folio);
		return action_result(pfn, MF_MSG_UNMAP_FAILED, MF_FAILED);
	}
```

    "thought": "**Refcount Shenanigans in Soft Offline and Memory Failure**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was that this wasn't very clear, so let's summarise the implications:

`soft_offline_in_use_page` has a few key functions: it checks if a folio is mapped or unmapped. It might isolate and migrate a hugetlb page using `folio_isolate_hugetlb()` and `migrate_pages`. If the migration is successful, it should free the hugetlb page. Crucially, if the folio is *not* mapped, it's invalidated.

Now, concerning refcounts, `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` adds a refcount via `folio_try_get()`. The caller is responsible for dropping it. `soft_offline_page` calls `soft_offline_in_use_page`, which is where things get interesting.  `soft_offline_page` sets `MF_SOFT_OFFLINE` using `get_hwpoison_page()`. `soft_offline_in_use_page` *does* call `folio_put(folio)`! That means any refcount gained is always dropped.  However, `memory_failure` itself *does not* drop this refcount, as the refcount is held to prevent the page from being reused.

For file hugetlb folios, the system has a pagecache refcount (equal to the number of pages in the folio) *plus* the memory failure refcount (1). When `remove_inode_hugepages` is called, say during file truncation/unlinking, the folio is removed from the page cache. `hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache(folio)` takes care of dropping the pagecache refcount. This leaves a refcount of 1 remaining, the memory failure refcount.

The critical piece of this discussion concerns how `soft_offline_page` and `memory_failure` interact. Specifically, the refcount logic within the patch itself:

```c
			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
				folio_put(folio);
			}
```

If we dropped the refcount here via `folio_put(folio)`, the folio will be dissolved. If the folio had been successfully soft-offlined, it's migrated and not in the page cache anyway. If the soft offline had *failed*, the folio's refcount would be dropped by `soft_offline_in_use_page`, so the original refcount will have been dropped. So there is no extra refcount.

The `soft_offline_page` calls `page_handle_poison()` under the condition of a successful migration. If `ret > 0`, it calls `soft_offline_in_use_page`. The interesting point is that *inside* `soft_offline_in_use_page`, the code calls `page_handle_poison()`.  If this process is successful, the refcount is always dropped!

Inside `page_handle_poison`, the function does `TestSetPageHWPoison`. The most important thing I found is: in `soft_offline_in_use_page`, for hugetlb (`huge` is true), the release flag will be false, so it sets `PG_hwpoison`, and it DOES NOT call `put_page`! Then, it calls `folio_put(folio)` and clears the `PG_HWPoison` bit and it drops the refcount.  So there are no remaining refcounts.

    "thought": "**Hugetlb Folio Poisoning and Refcount Management: A Deep Dive**

Okay, so I've been tracing the logic around handling hardware-poisoned hugetlb folios, and there are some critical refcount and page cache interactions to consider. Specifically, I'm examining how `remove_inode_hugepages` deals with these folios.

Initially, I see how `soft_offline_in_use_page` attempts to migrate a poisoned hugetlb folio. If migration succeeds, `page_handle_poison` is called *on the old folio*.  Crucially, for hugepages, `page_handle_poison` *doesn't* release the refcount.  That's because the isolation process (via `isolate_folio_to_list` or `folio_isolate_hugetlb`) initially grabs a refcount.  However, `soft_offline_in_use_page` *drops* the original refcount obtained from `get_any_page()` *before* migration. The `migrate_pages` function handles the refcount of the old folio itself by putting it and potentially freeing it.  If it's freed, the refcount is now 0. Thus, `page_handle_poison` effectively marks an unreferenced folio. When the refcount drops to zero in `migrate_pages`, it's freed back to the hugetlb pool.  The point here is that `remove_inode_hugepages` won't see this case because the folio is dissolved, or no longer in the page cache after the migration and freeing.

So, the only way I can see `remove_inode_hugepages` encountering a poisoned hugetlb folio is if: (1) it's a file-backed hugetlb folio and (2) `memory_failure` was called.  The latter will set `PG_hwpoison` and, via `me_huge_page()`, keep the folio in the page cache *and* increment a reference count.

Now, a key question becomes: is there a scenario where `PG_hwpoison` is set on a file hugetlb folio *in* the page cache, *but* the extra refcount isn't there?  I examined the `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` path.  It turns out, if `hwpoison_filter` fails, it *clears* `PG_hwpoison` and *puts* the folio, effectively handling that case. However, if `hwpoison_user_mappings` fails (which it might), the refcount *is* leaked, even though the `memory_failure` process returns an error. This leak is correct as `remove_inode_hugepages` can detect the situation.

Further, I realized that if `memory_failure` *succeeds* in setting `PG_hwpoison`, it takes that one additional refcount. If the folio has already been hwpoisoned, the next `memory_failure` will not add to the refcount because of the initial `TestSetPageHWPoison(p)` check! That first `memory_failure` with that initial refcount is key.

Therefore, under normal circumstances, if `PG_hwpoison` is set on a hugetlb folio in the page cache, it's *guaranteed* to have exactly *one* extra refcount, which is very important. This is because `memory_failure` only sets `PG_hwpoison` if `TestSetPageHWPoison` returns false and does not drop the refcount until `hwpoison_filter` is run.

However, I started thinking about edge cases. What if `remove_inode_single_folio` removes it from the page cache, and then the refcount is put? What if the page cache is truncated via `hugetlb_vmdelete_list`? That doesn't remove it from the pagecache. The `remove_inode_hugepages` does that.

Then, there's the patch's `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` part. `remove_inode_single_folio` removes it from the page cache and the patch drops the refcount. If the refcount drops to 0, it's dissolved, and *that's* why the temporary flag is needed! This temporary flag makes sure `free_huge_folio` dissolves rather than re-inserting the hugetlb folio into the pool. I'll need to check `free_huge_folio()` to confirm.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the HugeTLB HWPoison Handling: A Deep Dive into Refcount Races**

Okay, so I've been staring at this patch and the associated logic, and there's a serious potential for a refcount underflow when dealing with HWPoisoned HugeTLB pages, specifically during file truncation, unlink, or punch-hole operations.

Here's the gist of it: `free_huge_folio` eventually gets called when a HugeTLB page needs to be freed.  The current patch adds logic to handle HWPoisoned pages.  If a page is HWPoisoned, the `memory_failure` path sets `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` and increments its refcount (to prevent reuse). When that refcount goes to 0 it dissolves the hugepage entirely.

The problem lies in `remove_inode_hugepages`, which is called during truncate/unlink etc. This function checks if a folio is hwpoisoned: `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)`. If it *is* poisoned, it calls `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` and decrements the refcount with `folio_put(folio)`. But what if the refcount that was meant to be dropped by `memory_failure` is in the process of being put by `remove_inode_hugepages`, or has already been put? This is where the race condition occurs:

The critical point is that `memory_failure` *also* has a path that could decrement the refcount. If the `hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache(folio)` gets called by the `remove_inode_hugepages` first, that will clear the mapping. When `memory_failure` runs, it checks for a null mapping, it then, itself, puts the refcount, causing a double-put and a refcount underflow!  The original `folio_put(folio)` calls from both threads cause a refcount decrement that will go under zero!

Here's how this race can occur: `memory_failure` increments the refcount, sets `PG_hwpoison`, *then* tries to get the folio lock. But `remove_inode_hugepages` comes in, takes a lock *first* , removes the folio from the page cache (setting mapping to NULL), and *then* checks `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)`. Because it's hwpoisoned, it now puts the refcount with `folio_put(folio)`. So when `memory_failure` finally gets the lock, sees the mapping is NULL, it will also then put the folio, resulting in *two* `folio_put` operations.

Another scenario is if `memory_failure` already completed *before* `remove_inode_hugepages` runs. In that case, `memory_failure` *didn't* put the refcount. `remove_inode_hugepages` then comes in and *does* put it. But that's only because it sees the `PG_hwpoison` bit set.  This might *seem* correct, it is fundamentally flawed because it can't distinguish between the finished and running `memory_failure` threads.

And there's this `me_huge_page` situation. If `me_huge_page` is run, and the `folio`'s `mapping` is already NULL, due to the concurrent call of `remove_inode_hugepages`, it *also* executes a `folio_put(folio)`! That also results in a double-put or a use-after-free.

Here's the real kicker: `remove_inode_hugepages` *shouldn't* blindly drop the refcount simply because the `PG_hwpoison` flag is set. The HWPoison flag doesn't tell us if memory failure has a live refcount or not.  The extra refcount is only there to isolate it and ensure that `memory_failure` can handle the poisoned page. So we need to *carefully* manage when we drop that refcount.

Moreover, removing the folio from the page cache is necessary in *some* cases.  If the folio is removed from the page cache, then what is used is a HWPOISON swap entry. This enables a user process to get a `SIGBUS` with `BUS_MCEERR_AR` when they access that region.  So the poisoning persists in the correct form.

A key aspect of this problem is that HWPoisoned pages are *never* supposed to be put back on free lists; so the patch does the right thing by making it temporary (so that when its refcount hits zero it is dissolved). This prevents further allocations from an MCE.

    "thought": "**Analysis of a Potential Double Folio Put in Hugetlbfs and Memory Failure Handling**

Okay, so here's the deal: I've been digging into this hugetlbfs memory failure scenario, and I think I've found a critical race condition in how `remove_inode_hugepages` interacts with `memory_failure`. The core issue revolves around reference counting of folios, particularly those that are hardware-poisoned.

I've traced that `remove_inode_hugepages` grabs `hugetlb_fault_mutex_table`, which serializes it only with page faults, and it does *not* serialize with `memory_failure`. This lack of synchronization is where things get dicey. The current patch proposes handling hwpoisoned folios by setting them as "temporary" and putting the reference. I think that patch introduces a double put, not solves it.

Here's the problem: `memory_failure` and `remove_inode_hugepages` can run concurrently. If `memory_failure` runs, it *tries* to get a refcount and set the HWPoison flag on a folio and then acquire the folio lock. But, if `remove_inode_hugepages` comes in at the same time and gets the lock first, removes the folio from the page cache, and *then* puts the refcount unconditionally because of the `PG_hwpoison` flag, it's a double free! Then `memory_failure` comes along, sees that the folio's mapping is NULL (because `remove_inode_hugepages` cleared it), and it *also* puts the refcount!

The key is that `memory_failure` calls `me_huge_page`, which puts the refcount if `folio_mapping` is NULL. This is intended for anonymous folios, but the page cache removal makes a hugetlb folio look identical, resulting in a double `folio_put()`. The race window is small, but real: `memory_failure`'s `folio_try_get()` and setting `PG_hwpoison` before the lock.

It's tempting to try fixing this by putting the refcount *before* removing the folio from the pagecache or doing it inside `folio_lock`, but these approaches still lead to a double-put because `memory_failure` depends on `mapping == NULL`, regardless of the HWPoison state.

A successful `hwpoison_user_mappings` is critical for `memory_failure` to reach the double-put scenario, if this step fails, then the refcount is not put and `remove_inode_hugepages` putting it is correct. Since the file is being destroyed in most of the use-cases, it would be trivial for this step to succeed.

If `memory_failure` has already finished when `remove_inode_hugepages` runs, then the patch is fine, but the concurrency case is broken.

I need to confirm the sequence:
1.  `remove_inode_hugepages` iterates, finds a hwpoisoned folio.
2.  It calls `hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache()`.
3.  It sets `folio->mapping = NULL`.
4.  Then, due to the HWPoison flag, it calls `folio_put()`.
5.  Concurrent `memory_failure` grabs a reference, sets `PG_hwpoison`, then gets the lock, calls `me_huge_page`.
6.  `me_huge_page` checks `folio_mapping()`. Since it's NULL, it *also* calls `folio_put()`.
7.  Double put!

If `hwpoison_user_mappings` fails, then the refcount is NOT put in `memory_failure()` and the put in `remove_inode_hugepages()` would be correct.

The `fallocate` with punch hole case is handled correctly: the hwpoisoned folio is removed from the page cache, and a subsequent read returns zeroes, which is the correct POSIX behavior.

The potential solution is to securely check `folio_test_hwpoison`, but that's a problem for the author. I just need to report this potential race!

    "thought": "**Hugetlb Folio Accounting Leak in Hardware Poison Handling**

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at the flow around hardware-poisoned huge pages and how they're handled in the hugetlb system. The core of the problem seems to be in the transition of a folio to a "temporary" state and the subsequent accounting adjustments, or lack thereof.

First, `free_huge_folio` is the function of interest. I see a check for `folio_test_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` and a call to `remove_hugetlb_folio`. This bit is where my attention is drawn to the adjustment of the accounting that occurs in this function. The critical point is that the `remove_hugetlb_folio` function takes an `adjust_surplus` argument. Now, in the case of a "temporary" folio, `adjust_surplus` is set to `false`.

The crucial insight is that setting a folio as "temporary" implies it wasn't previously temporary. That means it was previously part of the hugetlb pool (either `nr_huge_pages` or `surplus_huge_pages`). The issue is, if we *change* a folio to temporary *without* adjusting the surplus accounting, we're bypassing the expected accounting path.

`remove_hugetlb_folio` decrements `nr_huge_pages` always, but it *only* decrements `surplus_huge_pages` if `adjust_surplus` is true. If the folio *was* a surplus folio *and* we set it as temporary, we're going to bypass the surplus decrement! This is a leak of surplus pages.

The situation is worsened by `memory_failure` which does, in fact, set the folio as temporary, in this case.  It seems the author is attempting to dissolve the page in this way.

The problem lies specifically in the `free_huge_folio` function: if a folio is *not* temporary, the code *checks* the surplus count and calls `remove_hugetlb_folio` with `adjust_surplus=true` if appropriate. However, if the page is *marked* temporary, it *always* calls `remove_hugetlb_folio` with `adjust_surplus=false`. This bypasses the surplus accounting.  This happens even if there was originally a surplus.

To reiterate: if we set the folio as temporary, we end up decrementing `nr_huge_pages`, but if it was originally a *surplus* page, we *don't* decrement `surplus_huge_pages`. This creates a leak.

Furthermore, `free_huge_folio` has no special handling for `PageHWPoison`. If a hwpoisoned folio is not temporary and not surplus, it's *enqueued back onto the free list*! This isn't good. `enqueue_hugetlb_folio` needs to be investigated because this could allow poisoned folios to potentially be reused. This looks like a serious problem.  This needs deeper investigation immediately.

    "thought": "**Refcount Mishap in Hugetlb Hardware Poisoning?**

Okay, let's think this through. I'm looking at `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison()`. Specifically, this part:

```c
	} else if (folio_test_hugetlb_migratable(folio)) {
		if (folio_try_get(folio)) {
			ret = MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED;
			count_increased = true;
		}
```

If `folio_try_get()` succeeds, `count_increased` gets set to `true`. This likely means we've bumped the refcount. Now, if we proceed to `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()` and end up in `hwpoison_user_mappings()`, then to `identify_page_state()`, and it triggers `me_huge_page()`. That's where things get interesting. `me_huge_page()` sets `extra_pins = true` if the folio has an associated mapping. This leads to `has_extra_refcount()` being called.

Here's the problem: `has_extra_refcount()` checks if `folio_ref_count() == folio_expected_ref_count() + extra_pins`. It *assumes* that `count_increased` from that earlier `folio_try_get()` call increased the reference count! But *where* does `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()` drop that additional reference? If it succeeds, it *doesn't* drop it, which makes sense - we want to isolate the page.

But wait a minute... What if `remove_inode_hugepages()` finds a Hugetlb folio that is `folio_test_hwpoison()` but *wasn't* poisoned by `memory_failure()`? Maybe something else set the HWPoison flag? I remember looking earlier and noticing that `arch/x86/kernel/cpu/mce/core.c` calls `SetPageHWPoison(p)` without taking a refcount.

Damn it, `SetPageHWPoison(p)` is being called on a `pfn_to_online_page()`. So we get the page online, and *then* later `memory_failure_queue()` or `memory_failure()` is called, right?

If the HWPoison bit is just set directly without a refcount, then `remove_inode_hugepages` is going to call `folio_put(folio)`! And *that* is going to UNDERFLOW the reference count! We have a potential race condition here. We've got to go verify `arch/x86/kernel/cpu/mce/core.c` again.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Use-After-Free Vulnerability**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial concern was a potential use-after-free scenario. If `arch/x86/kernel/cpu/mce/core.c` marks a page as `PageHWPoison` and that page later happens to be part of a hugetlb file undergoing deletion by `remove_inode_hugepages()`, the unconditional `folio_put(folio)` call in the latter could lead to a refcount underflow and a use-after-free, if the MCE code didn't take a reference.

But wait, does the MCE code *take* a refcount? No, it doesn't. Specifically, the MCE handler calls `SetPageHWPoison(p)` in `kill_me_maybe()` for action optional/required. Wait, no. It calls it in `do_machine_check` when recovering, but `memory_failure` is queued for action required / optional.

First, I see the core logic in `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`, where `get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` plays a crucial role. I need to pay close attention to the various return values and conditional logic.  It seems the critical path is when `get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` returns `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY` and the subsequent handling, specifically, what happens to the `MF_COUNT_INCREASED` reference, which represents a reference gained through `madvise`.

A significant point: if `get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` *doesn't* return `MF_HUGETLB_NON_HUGEPAGE`,  the `MF_COUNT_INCREASED` reference isn't dropped immediately! Also, it does NOT drop the reference with `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY`.  This implies a possible leak if the retry path is taken.

Now, if `MF_COUNT_INCREASED` *is* set, `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` drops the `madvise` reference, which is what I expect. However, if that flag is NOT set, `folio_try_get` can take a reference, which leads to `count_increased = true`. If `hugetlb_update_hwpoison` returns 0, then the reference is NOT dropped.

My next line of thought is how `identify_page_state` is related to `me_huge_page`, and if the page gets put in the page cache. If it does, then the reference is *not* dropped.

Here is the crux of the problem. What happens if the file backing the huge page is deleted? If the file is deleted, `remove_inode_hugepages` is called, which calls `remove_inode_single_folio`, which then, in a very specific scenario, may call `folio_put`.

The potential race condition is now clear. What if an MCE causes `SetPageHWPoison` to be called *without* a corresponding reference being taken by `memory_failure`? The MCE handler directly sets the `PG_hwpoison` flag. However, `memory_failure` will be queued by `memory_failure_queue`.

If `remove_inode_hugepages` is called *before* `memory_failure` actually runs, `remove_inode_hugepages` will find the `PG_hwpoison` flag set, and then incorrectly calls `folio_put`!

Let's summarize.
1.  An MCE occurs, and `SetPageHWPoison(p)` is called directly. No reference is increased.
2.  `memory_failure_queue()` queues `memory_failure`.
3.  *Before* `memory_failure()` runs, the hugetlb file is truncated, triggering `remove_inode_hugepages()`.
4.  `remove_inode_hugepages()` sees `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` as true.
5.  `folio_put(folio)` is called *without* any corresponding reference from `memory_failure` being taken.

This is a clear reference counting underflow vulnerability, leading to use-after-free or a negative reference count. I need to verify that `SetPageHWPoison` is never called without a reference being held. I need to make sure this race condition is addressed, potentially by holding a lock like `hugetlb_fault_mutex` throughout the critical section.

I was initially thinking it was called during the normal path through `kill_me_maybe()` which calls `memory_failure()`, but it seems it's actually triggered when recovering from a guest Machine Check Exception (MCE) around line 1711. This is where it gets interesting: the CPU core has already marked the guest as dead, and `SetPageHWPoison(p)` is used to mark a page as poisoned to prevent its addition to the free list when the guest terminates.

I need to be very precise here. If the page `p` happens to be a hugetlb folio, `SetPageHWPoison` correctly sets the `PG_hwpoison` bit. It's not just the head page; it's the exact subpage `p`. Now, the important question is how `folio_test_hwpoison` behaves. Does it check the entire folio, including the head page, or just the specific subpage? I remember that `PG_hwpoison` is marked with `PF_ANY`. So, `folio_test_hwpoison` *will* check this specific subpage. Crucially, the code in `do_machine_check` doesn't call `memory_failure()` in this case because the guest is being terminated; the page is simply marked.

Now, consider the scenario where the guest is using hugetlbfs for its memory, perhaps through `memory-backend-memfd` or directly with hugetlbfs. When the guest exits, QEMU will delete the hugetlb file which triggers the `remove_inode_hugepages()` function. Here's the critical point: `remove_inode_hugepages()` checks `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)`. If that test returns true, it will call `folio_put(folio)`. But the system didn't call `memory_failure()`! This page has never been properly "handled" with the standard hardware poisoning path. This is key, because `folio_try_get()` or `get_hwpoison_page()` were NEVER called! So `remove_inode_hugepages()` will call `folio_put(folio)` and drop a reference that doesn't exist, leading to a negative refcount and a potential use-after-free vulnerability!     "thought": "**Race Condition Discovered in Hugetlb HWPoison Handling**

Okay, let's break this down. I've stumbled upon something interesting when thinking about how hardware errors (specifically, those involving hugetlb folios) are handled. The key seems to be around how the `PG_hwpoison` flag is propagated and checked.

Initially, I observed that `SetPageHWPoison(p)` on a subpage of a hugetlb folio causes `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` to return `true`. Digging deeper into `include/linux/page-flags.h`, it becomes clear that `folio_test_hwpoison` solely checks `PageHWPoison` on the *head page* of the folio. `folio_contain_hwpoisoned_page` is more comprehensive, but it relies on `folio_test_has_hwpoisoned` for large folios.

Here's the crux: if an MCE (Machine Check Exception) detects an error on a *tail* page, `PG_hwpoison` is set on that *specific* tail page. `folio_test_hwpoison` will then erroneously return *false* because it only looks at the head page. However, the subsequent processing path through `memory_failure` and `hugetlb_update_hwpoison` *does* set `PG_hwpoison` on the *head* page via `folio_test_set_hwpoison(folio)`.

So, `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` is only *true* if the head page has had `PG_hwpoison` set. This is normally set via `memory_failure`. But if `remove_inode_hugepages` is called *before* `memory_failure` runs, then `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` will be *false*!

But what if the MCE hits the *head* page? Then, `SetPageHWPoison(p)` will set it on the *head* page. Therefore, `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` would be *true* even before `memory_failure` has a chance to run.

Now, here's where the race condition appears. If `remove_inode_hugepages` runs concurrently, and finds `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` true (because the head page was poisoned), it will call `folio_put(folio)`. But the reference wasn't taken because `memory_failure` hasn't run yet.

I suspect `SetPageHWPoison(p)` doesn't itself take a reference. My current thinking is that `memory_failure()` is responsible for the refcount increase. Consequently, there is now a race condition where `remove_inode_hugepages` underflows the refcount if it races with `memory_failure` on the head page! The timing is the key.

I need to confirm if either `memory_failure_queue` or `kill_me_maybe` are involved in the refcount management here. I must examine `memory_failure_queue` and the codepaths that are executed as a result.

    "thought": "**Analysis of a Hugetlb Folio Double-Free Vulnerability**

Okay, so I've been dissecting this hugetlb folio issue, and it looks like there's a serious race condition that can lead to a double-free. Let's break down my thinking:

First, `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` is just a flag setter for the hugetlb folio, nothing critical here. And then, `remove_inode_single_folio()` removes a folio from the page cache. Followed by a call to `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` and then a `folio_put(folio)` to drop an extra reference. My main concern is, is `memory_failure` *guaranteed* to run and pick up the reference before `remove_inode_hugepages` drops it?

`memory_failure` will take a reference through `folio_try_get(folio)`. If the folio is no longer migratable, could that fail? No, if it's already removed from the page cache, `folio_test_hugetlb_freed(folio)` will possibly be true! And in `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio()` it clears and frees the folio.

The commit message points to a problem: if a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, `me_huge_page()` (part of `memory-failure.c`) keeps the folio in the pagecache with an increased refcount, and `PG_hwpoison` set. Even after file deletion, the folio "leaks".

The root of the bug is that `me_huge_page` doesn't drop the `MF_COUNT_INCREASED` reference or its own `extra_pins`. This extra reference is added by `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`, which itself is called from within the `memory_failure` path.

Why does `me_huge_page` retain a reference? Because it doesn't want the page freed back to the buddy allocator because it's hardware-poisoned. But if it's in the page cache, reads will hit `PG_hwpoison` and return SIGBUS. If the file is deleted, the folio is removed from the pagecache *but* the extra reference from `memory_failure` remains – so, a leak! The author's patch tries to fix this by dropping the extra reference on file deletion, using `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio); folio_put(folio);`.

But is this reference *always* present? What if `madvise(MADV_HWPOISON)` is called? That calls `madvise_inject_error` -> `memory_failure`. Does `memory_failure` take a reference? Yes, `get_user_pages_fast` takes a reference, which `memory_failure` drops if `MF_COUNT_INCREASED` is set and it's pre-poisoned or if it's normal. For hugetlb pages, `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` takes an *extra* reference in `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison`. So, a *successful* `memory_failure` on a hugetlb page adds *one* extra reference. But if `memory_failure` *fails*?

If `hwpoison_user_mappings` fails, does it drop the reference? Let's check: it doesn't. The extra reference is *always* held if `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` succeeds. However, if `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` returns `MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED`, no extra reference is added! So one `memory_failure` = 1 extra reference.

Now, what if `remove_inode_hugepages` is called and drops a reference? It can't be called multiple times, because the page cache removal in `remove_inode_single_folio` makes it unavailable again. But, what if the folio is poisoned by `SetPageHWPoison(p)` in the MCE handler *before* `memory_failure` runs?

Here's the race:

1.  MCE handler sets `PG_hwpoison` (no refcount increase).

Okay, so here's the core of the issue as I see it, after meticulously tracing the code and all the scenarios. It all boils down to a race condition where a hugetlb folio can be marked as `PG_hwpoison` by the MCE handler *without* a corresponding reference being held, and `remove_inode_hugepages` is *unconditionally* dropping a reference based solely on the presence of that flag. That is the critical mistake that can be exploited and lead to the crash.

First, let's establish the context. When a hardware memory error (MCE) occurs, the `SetPageHWPoison` flag is set on the folio (either the head or tail page) through the MCE handler, which *doesn't* automatically increment the refcount. This asynchronous process, like `kill_me_maybe()` or `mce_handle_guest_mca()`, then queues `memory_failure` to run later. The memory failure process should hold one additional reference on the folio, so that `PG_hwpoison` can be set and the page can be taken offline. The problem occurs when the process ends (perhaps the file is deleted or the program exits) before the queued `memory_failure` function has the chance to run.

Now, let's step through `remove_inode_hugepages`. It uses `filemap_get_folios`, which takes *one* reference (stored in `fbatch`), and checks `folio_test_hwpoison`. Crucially, this check occurs *after* the page has been removed from the page cache. Because `PG_hwpoison` was set by the MCE handler, `folio_test_hwpoison` returns true, and the patch, unconditionally calls `folio_put(folio)`. But the folio was already taken by `filemap_get_folios` and it wasn't supposed to be freed. Because of the race, the folio *doesn't* actually have an extra reference held by `memory_failure`. That causes an underflow! Later on, at the end of the loop, `folio_batch_release(&fbatch)` is called, which calls `folio_put()` *again*, resulting in a double-free.

The patch *assumes* that `folio_test_hwpoison` *implies* that `memory_failure` *has* run and acquired an extra reference, but this isn't true. The MCE handler sets the flag *without* taking a reference. Or if it's set by memory failure, and `memory_failure` fails to get the reference (for example because the page is already gone), the code still goes ahead and sets `PG_hwpoison`.

In the scenario where `memory_failure` fails to obtain an additional reference, but still sets `PG_hwpoison`, `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` calls `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` which attempts a `folio_try_get`. If this fails, it *still* calls `hugetlb_update_hwpoison`, which then sets `PG_hwpoison`. This is a critical point: `PG_hwpoison` is set *even* if a reference isn't obtained. In `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`, `__page_handle_poison()` might *increment* the refcount (but only if it succeeds). But if it doesn't succeed, the code does *nothing* and the folio has `PG_hwpoison` set, but *no* extra reference. Then `remove_inode_hugepages` hits it, drops a reference, and we get the underflow and double-free.

The mutexes (specifically `hugetlb_fault_mutex`) are *irrelevant* because the MCE handler runs in NMI context, and `remove_inode_hugepages` *does not* hold the `folio_lock` at the moment of the check.

The `SetPageHWPoison` flag is set either on the head or tail page. If it is set on the head page, `folio_test_hwpoison` is obviously true, and we have the double free. If it's set on the tail page, then the check on the head page is false, and it isn't a problem *until* memory failure promotes the flag to the head page.

It's tempting to think that `remove_inode_hugepages` *always* calling `folio_put()` on `hwpoison` folios would fix a leak, but it's a very flawed fix. And if it's meant to be a fix, it seems really brittle because the presence of the `PG_hwpoison` flag is decoupled from the reference that `memory_

    "thought": "**Critical Analysis of Hugetlb Folio Handling in `remove_inode_hugepages`**

Okay, let's break this down. The core issue revolves around how hardware-poisoned (HWPoison) HugeTLB folios are handled during file truncation, specifically within the `remove_inode_hugepages` function. My primary concern is a potential double-free vulnerability stemming from incorrect reference counting.

First, I'm analyzing the expected behavior. When a HugeTLB folio is HWPoisoned, the system is supposed to "dissolve" it back into the buddy allocator, keeping the poisoned subpage isolated. The code in `__update_and_free_hugetlb_folio` does this correctly: the `PG_hwpoison` flag is transferred to the individual tail pages, and the remaining healthy pages are freed.  This is achieved by setting `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` and then calling `folio_put()`. The intent here is sound; we don't want the poisoned folio to stay in the HugeTLB pool, so we force it back to the buddy allocator.

The key question is when that refcount to 0 is dropped and the folio is freed: how is this intended? I'm examining how the extra reference is handled, specifically by the memory failure handling (`memory_failure`). If `memory_failure` determines that a page is HWPoisoned, it's supposed to take an extra reference on the folio, preventing it from being immediately freed. The intention is to keep the page pinned until the memory management can deal with its poisoned state.

However, the problem lies in the conditional `folio_put(folio)` call within the `remove_inode_hugepages` function. The code unconditionally decrements the refcount when `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` is true, regardless of whether `memory_failure` has *actually* taken an extra reference. This is a critical point. If `memory_failure` has run, it may or may not have taken an extra reference. If it did take the extra reference, then the extra `folio_put()` will prematurely drop the refcount. Meaning, `hugetlb` and `temporary` are somehow joined together in the macro expansion. That's a possibility, right?

Alright, to investigate this further I need to look for any usage of `temporary` specifically within the `mm/hugetlb.c` file to trace down the origin of the constituent part of `hugetlb_temporary`.

    "thought": "**Refcount Underflow and Double Free Vulnerability in Hugetlb Folio Handling**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was about how `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary` interacts with `folio->private` and the atomicity provided by `set_bit`. It looked safe at first glance, but that's just the surface. Setting `HPG_temporary` is fine; it just prevents the folio from being added to the hugetlb freelist upon freeing. The real issue arises from *when* `folio_put` is called.

The problem lies in the unconditional call to `folio_put(folio)` within `remove_inode_hugepages()` based on a check for `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)`. My analysis reveals a race condition. The MCE handler sets `PageHWPoison` (via `SetPageHWPoison`) asynchronously, potentially in an NMI or hardirq context without immediately acquiring an additional reference on the folio. That reference is only taken later by `memory_failure` via a workqueue.

This means there's a window where `folio_test_hwpoison` can be true *before* the extra reference from `memory_failure` has been acquired. If `remove_inode_hugepages` runs within this window, it will call `folio_put`, decrementing the refcount before it should.  This underflows the refcount.  Later, when the loop within `remove_inode_hugepages` completes, `folio_batch_release` is called, and a second `folio_put` occurs, leading to a double-free in the buddy allocator.

Furthermore, even if `memory_failure` is running concurrently, if `remove_inode_single_folio()` removes the folio from the page cache first, `me_huge_page()` will see `folio_mapping(folio) == NULL` and it will drop its own extra reference. In this case as well, `remove_inode_hugepages()` calling an extra `folio_put(folio)` will cause an underflow.

There's another potential issue regarding `remove_inode_single_folio`, but that function always returns true. Therefore, the refcount underflow is still the concern.

I am confident this refcount underflow is a real vulnerability, so I am going to report this concern.

The result is a double-free! The refcount is dropped twice for the *same* extra reference taken in step 3! This is a classic Use-After-Free/kernel panic in the making!

So, the issue is that both functions think they are responsible for putting the folio.

*   `remove_inode_hugepages` is thinking, "I'm deleting a poisoned folio from the page cache, so I must decrement its reference count."
*   `me_huge_page` is thinking, "This poisoned folio *isn't* in the page cache, so I must decrement its reference count."

This TOCTOU (Time-of-Check-to-Time-of-Use) scenario is the problem.

I verified that `remove_inode_hugepages` can indeed race with `memory_failure`. The check for `PG_hwpoison` is *outside* the folio lock, so that's where the race lives. Even worse, `remove_inode_hugepages` does *not* clear `PG_hwpoison`.

I thought about other scenarios.

I examined `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` again. Does it hold the `hugetlb_lock`? No, it drops it before returning.

Okay, so here's the situation as I see it. `hwpoison_user_mappings` seems innocuous at first – it simply returns `true` if a page is already unmapped, which is part of its job to handle hwpoison pages that may have been already unmapped, but then, if the page is truly unmapped, `memory_failure` continues into `identify_page_state`. This leads us to `me_huge_page`.

Now, if `remove_inode_hugepages` has *already* removed the folio from the page cache (e.g., due to a `fallocate` punch hole) *before* `me_huge_page` runs, then `folio_mapping(folio)` will return `NULL`. This triggers the anonymous hugetlb path in `me_huge_page`, causing it to call `folio_put(folio)`. But here's the kicker: If `remove_inode_hugepages` *also* called `folio_put(folio)` because the page had `PG_hwpoison` set, we have a double-free scenario, or a race condition between `memory_failure` and `remove_inode_hugepages`.

Let's break down the race condition:

1.  **Initial state:** A hugetlb file folio exists, mapped in the page cache with a refcount of N.
2.  **Thread A (memory\_failure):**
    *   Gets a refcount by calling `folio_try_get` and sets `PG_hwpoison`. Refcount becomes N+1.
    *   Blocks on `folio_lock`.
3.  **Thread B (remove\_inode\_hugepages):**
    *   Gets a batch of folios, and `remove_inode_single_folio` locks the folio. Refcount is now N+2.
    *   Removes the folio from the page cache. Refcount is now N+1.
    *   Unlocks the folio.
    *   Checks for the hwpoison flag. It's `TRUE` (because of Thread A's earlier action), so `remove_inode_hugepages` calls `folio_put(folio)`. Refcount is now N.
4.  **Thread A (memory\_failure) continues:**
    *   Acquires the `folio_lock`.
    *   `hwpoison_user_mappings` finds the folio is not mapped, and returns `true`.
    *   `identify_page_state` dispatches to `me_huge_page`.
    *   `me_huge_page` calls `folio_mapping(folio)`. Because T2 removed it from the page cache, `folio->mapping` is `NULL`.
    *   `me_huge_page` takes the anonymous hugetlb path.
    *   Unlocks the folio.
    *   Calls `folio_put(folio)`. Refcount becomes N-1. This is the double-free.

The key point is that `remove_inode_hugepages` assumes that a folio with `PG_hwpoison` has already been *fully* processed by `memory_failure`, with an extra refcount. However, it can race. The problem is `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` sets the `PG_hwpoison` *before* acquiring the `folio_lock`.

How do we fix this? Perhaps `remove_inode_hugepages` needs to check `PG_hwpoison` *inside* the `folio_lock`? If `memory_failure` hasn't acquired the lock yet, this won't help because `PG_hwpoison` would still be set. The issue is `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` sets this flag without holding the folio lock.

So, how can we prevent the double free? Ideally, `remove_inode_hugepages` should only drop the refcount if `me_huge_page` has already decided to keep the page (to fix the reported leak). The question is how to make `remove_inode_hugepages` know the outcome of the `memory_failure` path. Does `me_huge_page` set a flag after the truncate call? We need a way to synchronize!

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Hugetlbfs Double Free Vulnerability**

Okay, let's break this down. Examining `hugetlbfs_aops`, I see `.error_remove_folio` points to `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio`. Looking at that function: it returns 0, but crucially, it does absolutely NOTHING to remove the folio. This is the first red flag.

So, `truncate_error_folio` returns 0 for hugetlb folios, and the `error_remove_folio` operation essentially does nothing. If it does nothing, then `me_huge_page` sets `extra_pins = true`, and `truncate_error_folio` returns 0. Actually, wait! `truncate_error_folio` returns `MF_RECOVERED` if `err == 0`! No. `if (err != 0) ... else if (!filemap_release_folio(folio, GFP_NOIO)) ... else ret = MF_RECOVERED;`.

This means the folio is *never* deleted from the page cache by `memory-failure` because `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` does nothing! So it remains in the page cache with `PG_hwpoison` set, and with an increased reference count. This aligns perfectly with the commit message describing a leaked hugetlb folio.

Now, my understanding of the race condition is solid: Thread A triggers `memory-failure`, and Thread B calls `remove_inode_hugepages`.

1.  T1: `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` increments the refcount, sets `PG_hwpoison`.
2.  T2: `remove_inode_hugepages` starts.
3.  T2: Locks the folio.
4.  T2: Calls `hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache(folio)`. Now `mapping = NULL`.
5.  T2: Unlocks the folio.
6.  T2: Checks `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)`. True. Calls `folio_put(folio)` and drops the extra refcount.
7.  T1: Acquires `folio_lock(folio)`.
8.  T1: Runs `hwpoison_user_mappings`.
9.  T1: Runs `me_huge_page`.
10. T1: Sees `folio_mapping(folio) == NULL`.
11. T1: Takes the `else` branch in `me_huge_page`, unlocks, and calls `folio_put(folio)`.
12. DOUBLE FREE!

Can we actually trigger this? Absolutely. `remove_inode_hugepages` is called during file deletion or hole punching. If a user maps a hugetlb file, triggers a memory failure (UCE or `madvise`), and then concurrently punches a hole or deletes the file, we can get this race. Similarly, if `memory_failure` is caused by a hardware error, and the process handles SIGBUS by deleting the file (or another thread punches a hole), it's game over. The race condition occurs if the hole punch happens precisely between `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` and `folio_lock` in `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`. The double free will occur.

Wait, are there any locks held by both threads? `remove_inode_hugepages` holds `hugetlb_fault_mutex_table[hash]`. Does `memory_failure` take this mutex? No, `memory_failure` only acquires `hugetlb_lock` (a spinlock) inside `get_huge_page_for_hwpoison`, and then `folio_lock(folio)`. The *only* synchronization mechanism is `folio_lock(folio)`.

Can we prevent this? What if we check `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` within the `folio_lock`? Nope, that doesn't work, because `memory_failure` has already set `PG_hwpoison`.

Wait! This commit introduces `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)`. What does *that* do?

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Race Condition in HugeTLB Page Handling**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought process is centered around how the `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` function interacts with the handling of HugeTLB pages, especially concerning `free_huge_folio` and surplus accounting. The central question is: Does setting a folio as temporary correctly bypass surplus adjustments, and under what conditions is this necessary or correct?

My first concern revolves around scenarios where a file is deleted, a folio is freed, and how the system keeps track of the number of huge pages. I'm wondering if, after `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` is called, the subsequent call to `free_huge_folio` would incorrectly account for a "surplus" or "deficit" in the number of huge pages. It would seem that if the folio is already accounted for, setting it as temporary and bypassing surplus adjustments would lead to a leak or miscount.

Now, a race condition catches my attention. I'm seeing a potential for a race condition between `remove_inode_hugepages` and `memory_failure`. Both threads might try to manipulate the reference count of a folio, leading to a double free, which is definitely bad. 

My initial thought is to confirm that the code path leads to a double `folio_put`. I check `me_huge_page` for this, and it does indeed call `folio_put` if the mapping is NULL. So if `remove_inode_hugepages` calls `folio_put` because of hwpoison, and then `memory_failure` also calls `folio_put` when the mapping becomes NULL, this would be a double free.

I'm verifying the steps in the race condition. I'm able to show that the extra reference count is taken by `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` in the thread handling the memory failure. The other thread is removing the folio. There's a window where `PG_hwpoison` is true, the reference is elevated, but the folio isn't locked by `memory_failure`, giving `remove_inode_hugepages` a chance to call `folio_put`.

The problem is that `remove_inode_hugepages` locks the folio, removes it from the page cache, and then sees the `PG_hwpoison` flag. Since the flag is set, it calls `folio_put(folio)`. If that is unlocked *before* the other thread can grab it and handle it, there's going to be a double decrement of the refcount. I've confirmed that this indeed is the root cause: the double decrement will lead to either a refcount underflow or a use-after-free situation.

The next concern is the effect of `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)`. If `PG_hwpoison` is set, `remove_inode_hugepages` sets the folio temporary. If the refcount drops to 0, `free_huge_folio` will call `remove_hugetlb_folio` with `adjust_surplus` set to `false`. This means that we're skipping the global surplus count adjustment. This would leak the surplus accounting!

In summary, the race condition between `remove_inode_hugepages` and `memory_failure`, specifically when handling `PG_hwpoison`, leads to a double `folio_put`. Furthermore, setting a folio to temporary within this context results in incorrect surplus accounting. This will result in an underflow and then a panic.

    "thought": "**Analyzing `free_huge_folio` and its Impact on HugeTLB Page Management**

Okay, so I'm digging into `free_huge_folio` and how it handles hugetlb folios, specifically how it interacts with temporary and surplus pages, and I'm seeing something that needs more scrutiny. My understanding is that `free_huge_folio` uses `remove_hugetlb_folio` in different ways. If the folio is marked as temporary, it's removed without accounting for surplus, and then immediately freed via buddy. Makes sense.

But, if it's *not* a temporary folio, the function seems to *either* adjust surplus or put it back on the free list, depending. Here's where it gets interesting: what if we *artificially* mark a normally accounted folio as `temporary`? We completely bypass the accounting update. That seems like a potential accounting problem. We are skipping any surplus adjustment, AND not putting it back on the free list. So what happens?

Now, I'm thinking about hwpoisoned pages. In general, they're not put back on the free list. So, what happens with `free_huge_folio` and hwpoisoned folios? The context is that `free_huge_folio` is what is called when the refcount of the folio reaches zero. I need to be sure.

I see that `free_huge_folio` doesn't explicitly check `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)`. If it wasn't temporary, it would check the surplus and, if there were no surplus pages, put the folio on the free list. But that's a problem if the page is hwpoisoned. We don't want a poisoned page on the free list! We want the poisoned page to be "dissolved". Does "dissolving" mean that `h->max_huge_pages` gets adjusted?

And, if `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio` is called, it clears the hugetlb flag and then frees it using buddy. Does that also affect `max_huge_pages`? I need to check `__update_and_free_hugetlb_folio` to see what is happening under the hood. I suspect that there might be a subtle inconsistency here in how we handle temporary, surplus, and hwpoisoned pages, and I need to verify how `max_huge_pages` is treated in all these scenarios.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Hugetlb Folio Handling with Hardware Poisoning**

Okay, so I'm digging into this hugetlb folio code and the interaction with hardware poisoning.  Initially, it looks like `enqueue_hugetlb_folio` simply puts a folio on the free list, `h->hugepage_freelists[nid]`. But, hold on, if the folio is hwpoisoned, does `free_huge_folio` put it on the free list?

Let's trace: In `free_huge_folio`, it seems if the folio is temporary or surplus, it avoids the free list. Otherwise, it *does* call `enqueue_hugetlb_folio`, potentially putting a poisoned page on the free list. That seems like a bad idea. But, maybe the allocator skips poisoned folios? Nope, there's `free_hpage_work` and `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folios` to clean things up later.

Aha! `memory_failure` calls `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison`. It returns `MF_HUGETLB_FREED` if the folio is on the free list. And `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` *does* dissolve it if it's "freed". But! If the folio was in use when the poison happened, `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` *doesn't* dissolve it. It just marks it.

So, if a hugetlb folio is in use, gets hardware poisoned, *and then* the file it belongs to is deleted, it gets put on the free list, but stays there until `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` is called. But `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` is ONLY called if it was on the free list *at the time of memory failure* .

Thus, if it was in use when poisoned, the folio sits on the free list forever. That reduces the effective size of the pool and includes it in `h->free_huge_pages`. That's a problem!

Okay, now I see the reproducer. If you try to offline memory, it fails. Why? Because the refcount from `memory_failure` was never dropped. It leaked the page. So it was never free.

The patch author fixes this by `folio_put(folio)` and *also* `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)`. If it did just `folio_put`, the folio would be freed and dissolved.  `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` bypasses that and immediately calls `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio`.

If the folio was surplus, `remove_hugetlb_folio` is called with `adjust_surplus = false`. So, the `surplus_huge_pages` counter leaks!  And yes, a file hugetlb folio *can* be a surplus page, if the pool is exhausted and `nr_overcommit_huge_pages` allows it.

Let me verify this in `remove_hugetlb_folio`.. Yep, `adjust_surplus` being false definitely leaks the surplus counter. The standard `free_huge_folio` path handles surplus correctly, but `folio_test_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` takes precedence. By setting `temporary`, they've introduced a surplus counter leak!

So, `alloc_hugetlb_folio` calls `alloc_surplus_hugetlb_folio` if the persistent pool is empty.  If that folio is hwpoisoned and the file is deleted, `remove_inode_hugepages` sets `temporary`.  `folio_put` drops the refcount to zero. Then `free_huge_folio` sees `temporary`, calls `remove_hugetlb_folio(h, folio, false)`, and *doesn't* decrement `surplus_huge_pages`.  The counter is now permanently wrong.

Okay, let's break this down. Initially, I was confused. I saw that `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` simply returns 0. That's immediately suspect. `truncate_error_folio` calls this, and, for hugetlbfs, this zero return leads to just calling `filemap_release_folio`. But `filemap_release_folio` *doesn't* actually remove the folio from the page cache. `generic_error_remove_folio` does the page cache removal, but we're not using that here. So, if `hugetlbfs_error_remove_folio` returns 0, the folio *stays* in the page cache, which is what the author notes as a leak when the file is deleted.

Then, there's the patch. The patch includes `remove_inode_single_folio`, which *does* remove the folio. This implies the leak is due to *extra* references. The author notes that "memory failure" takes a reference. So, does memory failure *really* add an extra reference?

Let's look at `me_huge_page`.  This function calls `truncate_error_folio`, then unlocks the folio, and sets `extra_pins = true`. Then I tried to understand where this extra reference might originate. The key is in `get_hwpoison_page()` -> `__get_hwpoison_page()` -> `get_hwpoison_hugetlb_folio()` or `folio_try_get()`. Bingo, there's a folio increment in `folio_try_get()`. So, the memory failure code *is* taking a reference. `page_action()` then calls `me_huge_page`, and it *doesn't* `folio_put` on success. This means that, unlike regular page cache pages where `generic_error_remove_folio` drops the page cache's reference, in the hugetlbfs case, that extra reference from memory failure is held. The intention is to keep the poisoned page from being reused.

Now it's clear. For a hugetlb file-backed page, the page is kept in the page cache *plus* we have this extra reference from the memory failure. Then, even when the file is deleted (and `remove_inode_hugepages` is called), which *should* remove the folio from the page cache, the extra reference prevents its refcount from reaching zero. That's the leak.

Okay, let's look at the fix in the patch. I see the critical line:

```c
			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
				folio_put(folio);
			}
```

This is the key. If the folio has `PG_hwpoison`, it drops a reference. But, is this safe? Could `folio_test_hwpoison` be true *without* an extra reference from the memory failure? I think not. `PG_hwpoison` is set by `memory_failure()`. Memory failure will set `PG_hwpoison` *only* if it has successfully isolated/handled the page, which *also* means it kept an extra reference. So the conditional and `folio_put` should be correct, which drops the extra reference and fixes the leak.
