This\n\t * also prevents changes to the mapping of this pfn until\n\t * poison signaling is complete.\n\t */\n\tcookie = dax_lock_folio(folio);\n\tif (!cookie)\n\t\treturn -EBUSY;\n\n\tif (hwpoison_filter(&folio->page)) {\n\t\trc = -EOPNOTSUPP;\n\t\tgoto unlock;\n\t}\n\n\tswitch (pgmap->type) {\n\tcase MEMORY_DEVICE_PRIVATE:\n\tcase MEMORY_DEVICE_COHERENT:\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * TODO: Handle device pages which may need coordination\n\t\t * with device-side memory.\n\t\t */\n\t\trc = -ENXIO;\n\t\tgoto unlock;\n\tdefault:\n\t\tbreak;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * Use this flag as an indication that the dax page has been\n\t * remapped UC to prevent speculative consumption of poison.\n\t */\n\tSetPageHWPoison(&folio->page);\n\n\t/*\n\t * Unlike System-RAM there is no possibility to swap in a\n\t * different physical page at a given virtual address, so all\n\t * userspace consumption of ZONE_DEVICE memory necessitates\n\t * SIGBUS (i.e. MF_MUST_KILL)\n\t */\n\tflags |= MF_ACTION_REQUIRED | MF_MUST_KILL;\n\tcollect_procs(folio, &folio->page, &to_kill, true);\n\n\tunmap_and_kill(&to_kill, pfn, folio->mapping, folio->index, flags);\nunlock:\n\tdax_unlock_folio(folio, cookie);\n\treturn rc;\n}\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_FS_DAX\n/**\n * mf_dax_kill_procs - Collect and kill processes who are using this file range\n * @mapping:\taddress_space of the file in use\n * @index:\tstart pgoff of the range within the file\n * @count:\tlength of the range, in unit of PAGE_SIZE\n * @mf_flags:\tmemory failure flags\n */\nint mf_dax_kill_procs(struct address_space *mapping, pgoff_t index,\n\t\tunsigned long count, int mf_flags)\n{\n\tLIST_HEAD(to_kill);\n\tdax_entry_t cookie;\n\tstruct page *page;\n\tsize_t end = index + count;\n\tbool pre_remove = mf_flags & MF_MEM_PRE_REMOVE;\n\n\tmf_flags |= MF_ACTION_REQUIRED | MF_MUST_KILL;\n\n\tfor (; index < end; index++) {\n\t\tpage = NULL;\n\t\tcookie = dax_lock_mapping_entry(mapping, index, &page);\n\t\tif (!cookie)\n\t\t\treturn -EBUSY;\n\t\tif (!page)\n\t\t\tgoto unlock;\n\n\t\tif (!pre_remove)\n\t\t\tSetPageHWPoison(page);\n\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * The pre_remove case is revoking access, the memory is still\n\t\t * good and could theoretically be put back into service.\n\t\t */\n\t\tcollect_procs_fsdax(page, mapping, index, &to_kill, pre_remove);\n\t\tunmap_and_kill(&to_kill, page_to_pfn(page), mapping,\n\t\t\t\tindex, mf_flags);\nunlock:\n\t\tdax_unlock_mapping_entry(mapping, index, cookie);\n\t}\n\treturn 0;\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(mf_dax_kill_procs);\n#endif /* CONFIG_FS_DAX */\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE\n\n/*\n * Struct raw_hwp_page represents information about \"raw error page\",\n * constructing singly linked list from ->_hugetlb_hwpoison field of folio.\n */\nstruct raw_hwp_page {\n\tstruct llist_node node;\n\tstruct page *page;\n};\n\nstatic inline struct llist_head *raw_hwp_list_head(struct folio *folio)\n{\n\treturn (struct llist_head *)&folio->_hugetlb_hwpoison;\n}\n\nbool is_raw_hwpoison_page_in_hugepage(struct page *page)\n{\n\tstruct llist_head *raw_hwp_head;\n\tstruct raw_hwp_page *p;\n\tstruct folio *folio = page_folio(page);\n\tbool ret = false;\n\n\tif (!folio_test_hwpoison(folio))\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (!folio_test_hugetlb(folio))\n\t\treturn PageHWPoison(page);\n\n\t/*\n\t * When RawHwpUnreliable is set, kernel lost track of which subpages\n\t * are HWPOISON. So return as if ALL subpages are HWPOISONed.\n\t */\n\tif (folio_test_hugetlb_raw_hwp_unreliable(folio))\n\t\treturn true;\n\n\tmutex_lock(&mf_mutex);\n\n\traw_hwp_head = raw_hwp_list_head(folio);\n\tllist_for_each_entry(p, raw_hwp_head->first, node) {\n\t\tif (page == p->page) {\n\t\t\tret = true;\n\t\t\tbreak;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tmutex_unlock(&mf_mutex);\n\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\nstatic unsigned long __folio_free_raw_hwp(struct folio *folio, bool move_flag)\n{\n\tstruct llist_node *head;\n\tstruct raw_hwp_page *p, *next;\n\tunsigned long count = 0;\n\n\thead = llist_del_all(raw_hwp_list_head(folio));\n\tllist_for_each_entry_safe(p, next, head, node) {\n\t\tif (move_flag)\n\t\t\tSetPageHWPoison(p->page);\n\t\telse\n\t\t\tnum_poisoned_pages_sub(page_to_pfn(p->page), 1);\n\t\tkfree(p);\n\t\tcount++;\n\t}\n\treturn count;\n}\n\n#define\tMF_HUGETLB_FREED\t\t0\t/* freed hugepage */\n#define\tMF_HUGETLB_IN_USED\t\t1\t/* in-use hugepage */\n#define\tMF_HUGETLB_NON_HUGEPAGE\t\t2\t/* not a hugepage */\n#define\tMF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED\t3\t/* folio already poisoned */\n#define\tMF_HUGETLB_PAGE_PRE_POISONED\t4\t/* exact page already poisoned */\n#define\tMF_HUGETLB_RETRY\t\t5\t/* hugepage is busy, retry */\n/*\n * Set hugetlb folio as hwpoisoned, update folio private raw hwpoison list\n * to keep track of the poisoned pages.\n */\nstatic int hugetlb_update_hwpoison(struct folio *folio, struct page *page)\n{\n\tstruct llist_head *head;\n\tstruct raw_hwp_page *raw_hwp;\n\tstruct raw_hwp_page *p;\n\tint ret = folio_test_set_hwpoison(folio) ? MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED : 0;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Once the hwpoison hugepage has lost reliable raw error info,\n\t * there is little meaning to keep additional error info precisely,\n\t * so skip to add additional raw error info.\n\t */\n\tif (folio_test_hugetlb_raw_hwp_unreliable(folio))\n\t\treturn MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED;\n\thead = raw_hwp_list_head(folio);\n\tllist_for_each_entry(p, head->first, node) {\n\t\tif (p->page == page)\n\t\t\treturn MF_HUGETLB_PAGE_PRE_POISONED;\n\t}\n\n\traw_hwp = kmalloc_obj(struct raw_hwp_page, GFP_ATOMIC);\n\tif (raw_hwp) {\n\t\traw_hwp->page = page;\n\t\tllist_add(&raw_hwp->node, head);\n\t} else {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Failed to save raw error info.  We no longer trace all\n\t\t * hwpoisoned subpages, and we need refuse to free/dissolve\n\t\t * this hwpoisoned hugepage.\n\t\t */\n\t\tfolio_set_hugetlb_raw_hwp_unreliable(folio);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Once hugetlb_raw_hwp_unreliable is set, raw_hwp_page is not\n\t\t * used any more, so free it.\n\t\t */\n\t\t__folio_free_raw_hwp(folio, false);\n\t}\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\nstatic unsigned long folio_free_raw_hwp(struct folio *folio, bool move_flag)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * hugetlb_vmemmap_optimized hugepages can't be freed because struct\n\t * pages for tail pages are required but they don't exist.\n\t */\n\tif (move_flag && folio_test_hugetlb_vmemmap_optimized(folio))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\t/*\n\t * hugetlb_raw_hwp_unreliable hugepages shouldn't be unpoisoned by\n\t * definition.\n\t */\n\tif (folio_test_hugetlb_raw_hwp_unreliable(folio))\n\t\treturn 0;\n\n\treturn __folio_free_raw_hwp(folio, move_flag);\n}\n\nvoid folio_clear_hugetlb_hwpoison(struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tif (folio_test_hugetlb_raw_hwp_unreliable(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\tif (folio_test_hugetlb_vmemmap_optimized(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\tfolio_clear_hwpoison(folio);\n\tfolio_free_raw_hwp(folio, true);\n}\n\n/*\n * Called from hugetlb code with hugetlb_lock held.\n */\nint __get_huge_page_for_hwpoison(unsigned long pfn, int flags,\n\t\t\t\t bool *migratable_cleared)\n{\n\tstruct page *page = pfn_to_page(pfn);\n\tstruct folio *folio = page_folio(page);\n\tbool count_increased = false;\n\tint ret, rc;\n\n\tif (!folio_test_hugetlb(folio)) {\n\t\tret = MF_HUGETLB_NON_HUGEPAGE;\n\t\tgoto out;\n\t} else if (flags & MF_COUNT_INCREASED) {\n\t\tret = MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED;\n\t\tcount_increased = true;\n\t} else if (folio_test_hugetlb_freed(folio)) {\n\t\tret = MF_HUGETLB_FREED;\n\t} else if (folio_test_hugetlb_migratable(folio)) {\n\t\tif (folio_try_get(folio)) {\n\t\t\tret = MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED;\n\t\t\tcount_increased = true;\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tret = MF_HUGETLB_FREED;\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\tret = MF_HUGETLB_RETRY;\n\t\tif (!(flags & MF_NO_RETRY))\n\t\t\tgoto out;\n\t}\n\n\trc = hugetlb_update_hwpoison(folio, page);\n\tif (rc >= MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED) {\n\t\tret = rc;\n\t\tgoto out;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * Clearing hugetlb_migratable for hwpoisoned hugepages to prevent them\n\t * from being migrated by memory hotremove.\n\t */\n\tif (count_increased && folio_test_hugetlb_migratable(folio)) {\n\t\tfolio_clear_hugetlb_migratable(folio);\n\t\t*migratable_cleared = true;\n\t}\n\n\treturn ret;\nout:\n\tif (count_increased)\n\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\n/*\n * Taking refcount of hugetlb pages needs extra care about race conditions\n * with basic operations like hugepage allocation/free/demotion.\n * So some of prechecks for hwpoison (pinning, and testing/setting\n * PageHWPoison) should be done in single hugetlb_lock range.\n * Returns:\n *\t0\t\t- not hugetlb, or recovered\n *\t-EBUSY\t\t- not recovered\n *\t-EOPNOTSUPP\t- hwpoison_filter'ed\n *\t-EHWPOISON\t- folio or exact page already poisoned\n *\t-EFAULT\t\t- kill_accessing_process finds current->mm null\n */\nstatic int try_memory_failure_hugetlb(unsigned long pfn, int flags, int *hugetlb)\n{\n\tint res, rv;\n\tstruct page *p = pfn_to_page(pfn);\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\tunsigned long page_flags;\n\tbool migratable_cleared = false;\n\n\t*hugetlb = 1;\nretry:\n\tres = get_huge_page_for_hwpoison(pfn, flags, &migratable_cleared);\n\tswitch (res) {\n\tcase MF_HUGETLB_NON_HUGEPAGE:\t/* fallback to normal page handling */\n\t\t*hugetlb = 0;\n\t\treturn 0;\n\tcase MF_HUGETLB_RETRY:\n\t\tif (!(flags & MF_NO_RETRY)) {\n\t\t\tflags |= MF_NO_RETRY;\n\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn action_result(pfn, MF_MSG_GET_HWPOISON, MF_IGNORED);\n\tcase MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED:\n\tcase MF_HUGETLB_PAGE_PRE_POISONED:\n\t\trv = -EHWPOISON;\n\t\tif (flags & MF_ACTION_REQUIRED)\n\t\t\trv = kill_accessing_process(current, pfn, flags);\n\t\tif (res == MF_HUGETLB_PAGE_PRE_POISONED)\n\t\t\taction_result(pfn, MF_MSG_ALREADY_POISONED, MF_FAILED);\n\t\telse\n\t\t\taction_result(pfn, MF_MSG_HUGE, MF_FAILED);\n\t\treturn rv;\n\tdefault:\n\t\tWARN_ON((res != MF_HUGETLB_FREED) && (res != MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED));\n\t\tbreak;\n\t}\n\n\tfolio = page_folio(p);\n\tfolio_lock(folio);\n\n\tif (hwpoison_filter(p)) {\n\t\tfolio_clear_hugetlb_hwpoison(folio);\n\t\tif (migratable_cleared)\n\t\t\tfolio_set_hugetlb_migratable(folio);\n\t\tfolio_unlock(folio);\n\t\tif (res == MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED)\n\t\t\tfolio_put(folio);\n\t\treturn -EOPNOTSUPP;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * Handling free hugepage.  The possible race with hugepage allocation\n\t * or demotion can be prevented by PageHWPoison flag.\n\t */\n\tif (res == MF_HUGETLB_FREED) {\n\t\tfolio_unlock(folio);\n\t\tif (__page_handle_poison(p) > 0) {\n\t\t\tpage_ref_inc(p);\n\t\t\tres = MF_RECOVERED;\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tres = MF_FAILED;\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn action_result(pfn, MF_MSG_FREE_HUGE, res);\n\t}\n\n\tpage_flags = folio->flags.f;\n\n\tif (!hwpoison_user_mappings(folio, p, pfn, flags)) {\n\t\tfolio_unlock(folio);\n\t\treturn action_result(pfn, MF_MSG_UNMAP_FAILED, MF_FAILED);\n\t}\n\n\treturn identify_page_state(pfn, p, page_flags);\n}\n\n#else\nstatic inline int try_memory_failure_hugetlb(unsigned long pfn, int flags, int *hugetlb)\n{\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\nstatic inline unsigned long folio_free_raw_hwp(struct folio *folio, bool flag)\n{\n\treturn 0;\n}\n#endif\t/* CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE */\n\n/* Drop the extra refcount in case we come from madvise() */\nstatic void put_ref_page(unsigned long pfn, int flags)\n{\n\tif (!(flags & MF_COUNT_INCREASED))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tput_page(pfn_to_page(pfn));\n}\n\nstatic int memory_failure_dev_pagemap(unsigned long pfn, int flags,\n\t\tstruct dev_pagemap *pgmap)\n{\n\tint rc = -ENXIO;\n\n\t/* device metadata space is not recoverable */\n\tif (!pgmap_pfn_valid(pgmap, pfn))\n\t\tgoto out;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Call driver's implementation to handle the memory failure, otherwise\n\t * fall back to generic handler.\n\t */\n\tif (pgmap_has_memory_failure(pgmap)) {\n\t\trc = pgmap->ops->memory_failure(pgmap, pfn, 1, flags);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Fall back to generic handler too if operation is not\n\t\t * supported inside the driver/device/filesystem.\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (rc != -EOPNOTSUPP)\n\t\t\tgoto out;\n\t}\n\n\trc = mf_generic_kill_procs(pfn, flags, pgmap);\nout:\n\t/* drop pgmap ref acquired in caller */\n\tput_dev_pagemap(pgmap);\n\tif (rc != -EOPNOTSUPP)\n\t\taction_result(pfn, MF_MSG_DAX, rc ? MF_FAILED : MF_RECOVERED);\n\treturn rc;\n}\n\n/*\n * The calling condition is as such: thp split failed, page might have\n * been RDMA pinned, not much can be done for recovery.\n * But a SIGBUS should be delivered with vaddr provided so that the user\n * application has a chance to recover. *folio)\n{\n\tLIST_HEAD(tokill);\n\n\tfolio_lock(folio);\n\tcollect_procs(folio, p, &tokill, flags & MF_ACTION_REQUIRED);\n\tfolio_unlock(folio);\n\n\tkill_procs(&tokill, true, pfn, flags);\n}\n\nint pfn_space->node.last))\n\t\tinterval_tree_remove(&pfn_space->node, &pfn_space_itree);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(unregister_pfn_address_space);\n\nstatic void add_to_kill_pgoff(struct task_struct *tsk,\n\t\t\t      struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t      struct list_head *to_kill,\n\t\t\t      pgoff_t pgoff)\n{\n\tstruct to_kill *tk;\n\n\ttk = kmalloc_obj(*tk, GFP_ATOMIC);\n\tif (!tk) to_kill);\n}\n\n/*\n * Collect processes when the error hit a PFN not backed by struct page.\n */\nstatic void collect_procs_pfn(struct pfn_address_space *pfn_space,\n\t\t\t      unsigned long pfn, pfn_space->mapping;\n\n\ti_mmap_lock_read(mapping);\n\trcu_read_lock();\n\tfor_each_process(tsk) {\n\t\tstruct task_struct *t = tsk;\n\n\t\tt = task_early_kill(tsk, true);\n\t\tif (!t)\n\t\t\tcontinue;\n\t\tvma_interval_tree_foreach(vma, &mapping->i_mmap, 0, ULONG_MAX) {\n\t\t\tpgoff_t pgoff;\n\n\t\t\tif (vma->vm_mm == t->mm &&\n\t\t\t    !pfn_space->pfn_to_vma_pgoff(vma, pfn, &pgoff))\n\t\t\t\tadd_to_kill_pgoff(t, vma, to_kill, pgoff);\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\trcu_read_unlock();\n\ti_mmap_unlock_read(mapping);\n}\n\n/**\n * memory_failure_pfn - Handle memory failure on a page not PFN does not belong to any address space mapping.\n */\nstatic int memory_failure_pfn(unsigned long pfn, int flags)\n{\n\tstruct interval_tree_node *node;\n\tLIST_HEAD(tokill);\n\n\tscoped_guard(mutex, &pfn_space_lock) {\n\t\tbool mf_handled = false;\n\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Modules registers with MM the address space mapping to\n\t\t * the device memory they manage. Iterate to identify\n\t\t * exactly which address space has mapped to this failing\n\t\t * PFN.\n\t\t */\n\t\tfor (node = interval_tree_iter_first(&pfn_space_itree, pfn, pfn); node;\n\t\t     node = interval_tree_iter_next(node, pfn, pfn)) {\n\t\t\tstruct pfn_address_space *pfn_space =\n\t\t\t\tcontainer_of(node, struct pfn_address_space, node);\n\n\t\t\tcollect_procs_pfn(pfn_space, pfn, &tokill);\n\n\t\t\tmf_handled = true;\n\t\t}\n\n\t\tif (!mf_handled)\n\t\t\treturn action_result(pfn, MF_MSG_PFN_MAP, MF_IGNORED);\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * Unlike System-RAM there is no possibility to swap in a different\n\t * physical page at a given virtual address, so all userspace\n\t * consumption of direct PFN memory necessitates SIGBUS (i.e.\n\t * MF_MUST_KILL)\n\t */\n\tflags |= MF_ACTION_REQUIRED | MF_MUST_KILL;\n\n\tkill_procs(&tokill, true, pfn, flags);\n\n\treturn action_result(pfn, MF_MSG_PFN_MAP, MF_RECOVERED);\n}\n\n/**\n * memory_failure - Handle memory failure of a page.\n * @pfn: Page Number of the corrupted page\n * @flags: fine tune action taken\n *\n * This function is called by the low level machine check code\n * of an architecture when it detects hardware memory corruption\n * of a page. It tries its best to recover, which includes\n * dropping pages, killing processes etc.\n *\n * The function is primarily of use for corruptions that\n * happen outside the current execution context (e.g. when\n * detected by a background scrubber)\n *\n * Must run in process context (e.g. a work queue) with interrupts\n * enabled and no spinlocks held.\n *\n * Return:\n *   0             - success,\n *   -ENXIO        - memory not managed by the kernel\n *   -EOPNOTSUPP   - hwpoison_filter() filtered the error event,\n *   -EHWPOISON    - the page was already poisoned, potentially\n *                   kill process,\n *   other negative values - failure.\n */\nint memory_failure(unsigned long pfn, int flags)\n{\n\tstruct page *p;\n\tstruct folio *folio;\n\tstruct dev_pagemap pfn);\n\n\tmutex_lock(&mf_mutex);\n\n\tif (!(flags & MF_SW_SIMULATED))\n\t\thw_memory_failure = true;\n\n\tp = pfn_to_online_page(pfn);\n\tif (!p) {\n\t\tres = arch_memory_failure(pfn, flags);\n\t\tif (res == 0)\n\t\t\tgoto unlock_mutex;\n\n\t\tif (!pfn_valid(pfn) && !arch_is_platform_page(PFN_PHYS(pfn))) {\n\t\t\t/*\n\t\t\t * The PFN is not backed by struct page.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\tres =     "thought": "**Hugetlb Folio Reference Leak with HWPoison**

Okay, so I've been tracing the logic around handling hardware-poisoned hugetlb folios. It seems there's a reference leak in specific scenarios. My primary concern is with the extra reference count added by `memory_failure`.

I've determined that in the `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` function, when handling a memory failure on a hugetlb folio, `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` can increment the folio's reference count if the folio is migratable. Following this, `me_huge_page`, the action handler, is called. It tries to truncate the page, but since `error_remove_folio` for hugetlbfs is a no-op, the folio remains in the page cache with an *extra* reference count. `me_huge_page` doesn't drop that extra reference.

So, a hwpoisoned hugetlb folio has its original page cache reference *plus* one extra reference from `memory_failure`. When the hugetlbfs file is deleted and `remove_inode_hugepages()` attempts to free the folio, the page cache's reference is dropped. However, the extra reference remains, and the folio is leaked. The patch addresses this by adding a check for `PG_hwpoison` and dropping the extra reference.

But the real complexity lies in the details. I’ve explored what happens when `memory_failure` is called multiple times on the *same* folio. The first call sets `PG_hwpoison` and *does* keep the reference. The second call, though, it returns `MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED`, and *drops* the reference. That's a critical optimization! That makes it so that, at the end of the day, there is only *one* extra reference.

I've also examined cases where the memory failure logic fails before adding the extra reference, or if `hwpoison_user_mappings` or `identify_page_state` fail. In these cases, even if these early failures happen, it does not drop the refcount. Thus, those are also potential leaks, and the patch fixing the leak is correct.

There's the unusual path of a hugetlb folio that has `PG_hwpoison` set, but *without* an extra reference. This happens when the `soft_offline_page` function handles hugetlb pages, because the extra reference is dropped early on. This can happen.

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Proposed Patch: Potential Double-Free and Use-After-Free Vulnerabilities**

Okay, so I've been dissecting this patch and I've identified some pretty nasty bugs. It seems there are two major issues here, both related to the interaction of hardware poisoning and hugetlb page removal, and the concurrency with `memory_failure`.

The first big problem is that the `PG_hwpoison` flag on a hugetlb folio doesn't reliably indicate the presence of an extra reference. There are scenarios, particularly during the failure paths of `soft_offline_page`, where `PG_hwpoison` can be set, but the reference taken by `get_hwpoison_page` is dropped. This can happen if the page is filtered by `hwpoison_filter` or if migration fails. So, when `remove_inode_hugepages` later comes along and finds that `PG_hwpoison` is set, it blindly calls `folio_put(folio)`. But the reference might not exist! This leads to a refcount underflow and a use-after-free because `folio_put()` is called when it shouldn't.

The second, and even more critical, issue is a race condition. The patch's check for `PG_hwpoison` and the subsequent `folio_put()` call happen *after* `remove_inode_single_folio` unlocks the folio. This creates a window of vulnerability if a concurrent `memory_failure` happens to run in between. `memory_failure` can acquire a reference to the poisoned folio when it sees the page (while `remove_inode_hugepages` is happening). `memory_failure` might be in the process of dealing with the folio. Now, `remove_inode_hugepages` notices the `PG_hwpoison` flag and *steals* that reference. Since `remove_inode_hugepages` doesn't have the folio lock, it's not even aware of what `memory_failure` is trying to do. This results in the folio being freed prematurely. By the time `memory_failure` comes back to use the folio, it will try to access freed memory, causing a use-after-free, likely a crash. The use-after-free can also happen during `folio_batch_release` at the end of the `remove_inode_hugepages` function. This is a very serious concurrency bug.

I've also examined the other operations and `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary`. There doesn't seem to be a problem associated with that particular operation in the context of this issue.

    "thought": "**Refcount Underflow in Hugetlb Page Poisoning**

Okay, here's the breakdown of my thought process regarding this hugetlb page poisoning issue, as I understand it.

The problem lies in how hardware errors are handled with hugetlb folios, specifically in the interaction between `memory_failure`, the page cache, and the folio's refcount. The core assumption the original code makes is that if a folio has `PG_hwpoison` set, then the `memory_failure` path will *always* have added an extra refcount to it. This assumption is WRONG.

I started by tracing the critical code path: `folio_clear_hugetlb_migratable` within `free_huge_folio`. I quickly realized that the *migratable* state of a hugetlb folio is key. A file-backed hugetlb folio *starts* as migratable, gets poisoned via `memory_failure`. The goal is to safely remove it from the page cache.

The critical insight is that there are circumstances where `memory_failure` *can* set `PG_hwpoison` on a hugetlb folio *without* incrementing its refcount. This happens when the folio is in a state that prevents `folio_try_get()` from succeeding within `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison`. It is particularly true when a hugetlb folio is being isolated for migration and a hardware error occurs during that critical window.

Here's the chain of events that leads to the race condition:

1.  A hugetlb folio is in the page cache and, due to some factor, becomes isolated or non-migratable.
2.  A hardware error happens, and `memory_failure` is called.
3.  Because the folio is isolated (e.g., in compaction), it's not migratable and `folio_try_get()` fails. The return code is `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY`.
4.  `hugetlb_update_hwpoison` is called, and the page is poisoned (`PG_hwpoison` is set).
5.  `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` does NOT increment the refcount in this situation; it just returns a retry.
6.  The migration/compaction completes, and the folio becomes migratable again.
7.  Later, `remove_inode_hugepages` is called, e.g. from deleting the file. The critical point here is that `remove_inode_single_folio` removes the folio from the page cache. It checks if the folio is poisoned: `folio_test_hwpoison`. This is TRUE!
8.  `remove_inode_hugepages` then incorrectly assumes that the folio's refcount was increased by `memory_failure`. The folio's extra refcount is dropped with the call to `folio_put`.
9. The folio's final refcount drops to 0!
10. `folio_batch_release` is subsequently called on the freed folio, resulting in a use-after-free.

The key is `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY` when the folio is not migratable. In this scenario, `memory_failure` sets the poison flag but does NOT hold a reference.

I thoroughly investigated other potential refcount manipulations, particularly if `hwpoison_filter` filtered the folio. I also considered other calls which could bypass the normal refcounting behavior. It became clear that the `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY` case is the most straightforward, and likely, the most common race condition.

I meticulously examined the code, including the interaction between `memory_failure`, `hugetlb_update_hwpoison`, and `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`, to isolate this precise sequence of events.

The patch author correctly identifies the issue as a refcount leak. The fix aims to decrement the extra refcount the "memory-failure" path has added. However, it's based on an incorrect premise that the extra refcount always exists. The patch's fundamental assumption is flawed and leads to this use-after-free vulnerability, which is caused by dropping the folio's refcount prematurely.

    "thought": "**Analysis of a Hugetlb Memory Poisoning Bug and Patch**

Okay, so I've been digging into this hugetlb memory poisoning issue and a proposed patch, and I've found a couple of pretty nasty flaws. It all starts with the way `get_user_pages_fast` and `madvise_inject_error` interact, ultimately calling `memory_failure`. The crux of the problem boils down to how these functions handle the page refcounts and the `PG_hwpoison` flag, especially in the context of hugetlb folios.

My initial thought was, "Aha! `get_user_pages_fast` increments the refcount, `memory_failure` gets the reference." But it turns out it's more subtle. When `madvise_inject_error` calls `memory_failure`, it *passes* the reference, expecting `memory_failure` to handle it. The important part is that `memory_failure` *might* keep the reference, depending on success or failure and what it is, and then the critical case of *hugetlb* pages.

For hugetlb pages, `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` calls `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison`. This function checks if `MF_COUNT_INCREASED` is set, and if it is, it increments a counter and gets a folio reference. But then it calls `hugetlb_update_hwpoison`, which atomically sets `PG_hwpoison`. Crucially, if there's an error later in `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`, it can *drop* the reference, or *keep* it, and this is where it starts to go wrong.

The patch's logic in `remove_inode_hugepages` is, "If `PG_hwpoison` is set, `folio_put` the folio!" But there's a huge assumption there: That if `PG_hwpoison` is set, `memory_failure` has *actually* acquired an extra reference. This is *not* always the case.

**The first major problem I see is a Use-After-Free due to a race condition:** The `remove_inode_hugepages` function, when it finds `PG_hwpoison` set, *unconditionally* calls `folio_put`. It doesn't care if `memory_failure` is still actively using the folio! There's no synchronization here, it's lockless! This means if `memory_failure` set the flag, but is still *doing stuff* with the folio, `remove_inode_hugepages` is *stealing* that extra refcount! This drops the reference from `fbatch`, or reduces the refcount to zero, which then causes a UAF. This is *horrendous*.

**The second massive issue is that refcount underflows can happen if a hugetlb folio is temporarily isolated:** The `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY` case. If a hugetlb folio is isolated during migration or other maintenance, `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` might *set* `PG_hwpoison` but *not* increment the folio's refcount. The `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY` will go back from `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` as ignored. This happens if `folio_test_hugetlb_migratable(folio)` is false. The refcount isn't increased. Then, later, the file is unlinked, `remove_inode_hugepages` runs, *sees* `PG_hwpoison`, and calls `folio_put`!  BOOM! A refcount underflow!

I see that `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` is called unconditionally. I can't find its definition but it probably is not safe to call without any locks as modifying folio state usually requires `folio_lock` or specific synchronization.

The commit description says the goal is to correctly handle the reference count.  However, the code itself doesn't guarantee this and these flaws show the assumption is definitely incorrect. I need to be sure that the patch either: synchronizes properly to prevent the UAF, or, doesn't try to drop a reference unless it *knows* `memory_failure` has completely finished with the folio. Otherwise, it is going to cause a lot of pain and crashes. The key seems to be the assumption that `PG_hwpoison` indicates a valid reference, which is wrong.

Okay, after a deep dive, I've identified a serious flaw in how hardware-poisoned hugetlb folios are handled, leading to potential Use-After-Free vulnerabilities. The key lies in the interaction between `remove_inode_hugepages` and `memory_failure`, particularly around refcount management and locking.

Here's the breakdown, in my current understanding:

**Concern 1: Unconditional Reference Drop Causes Use-After-Free**

The primary issue is a classic race condition. The `remove_inode_hugepages` function, when dealing with a hardware-poisoned folio (detected via `folio_test_hwpoison`), unconditionally calls `folio_put(folio)` *after* clearing the folio from the pagecache. This drop of refcount could happen if `memory_failure()` is running concurrently, has just obtained a reference to the folio, and set `PG_hwpoison`. Crucially, there's no synchronization to prevent this "stealing" of the reference.

Here's the sequence:

1.  `memory_failure()`: Gets an extra reference on the poisoned folio and sets `PG_hwpoison`.
2.  `remove_inode_hugepages()`: Removes the folio from the pagecache, calls `folio_test_hwpoison()`. It *sees* that `PG_hwpoison` is set and proceeds to call `folio_put()` immediately.
3.  The folio gets freed immediately at point 2 when the folio refcount becomes zero (when `fbatch` is released).
4. `memory_failure()` is still running and calls `folio_lock(folio)` on a now-freed folio.

This leads to a guaranteed Use-After-Free, since `memory_failure()` is now operating on an already freed page.

**Concern 2: Refcount Underflow for Isolated Folios**

Even without concurrent `memory_failure` calls, a subtle scenario exists where the folio's refcount could be underflowed. If a hardware memory error occurs on a hugetlb folio that is temporarily isolated, for example, during compaction, the `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` function *may* set `PG_hwpoison` without incrementing the refcount (e.g., in the `MF_HUGETLB_RETRY` case).

    "thought": "**Hugetlb Memory Failure: A Reference Counting Conundrum**

Okay, let's break this down. My understanding of the code is that `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` behaves in a specific way. If it sees `MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED`, it just returns it and doesn't call `folio_put(folio)` *unless* there's an error. So, essentially, it doesn't decrement the reference count in that scenario.

Then, inside `try_memory_failure_hugetlb`, if `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` returns `MF_HUGETLB_IN_USED`, the code proceeds. If it *can* lock the folio, it continues, and crucially, there is no `folio_put(folio)` call *after* the successful hardware poisoning. That means `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` leaves the reference count incremented by the initial `try_get_folio()` in `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison`. So, by design, `memory_failure` for hugetlb *leaks* a reference.

The patch seems to try to balance this by dropping a reference in the page cache removal, but I'm questioning that logic, especially if we look at `MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED`. If the folio is *already* poisoned (as indicated by `hugetlb_update_hwpoison` returning `MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED`), then `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` *does* decrement the reference count via the `folio_put(folio)` call! So, a pre-poisoned folio *doesn't* leak a reference in `memory_failure`.

The double madvise scenario, however, is what really brings this into focus. On the first call to `madvise(MADV_HWPOISON)`: a reference is taken, it's poisoned, it succeeds, and the reference *is* leaked. On the second call, the `hugetlb_update_hwpoison` function now sees the folio as pre-poisoned. So, `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison` takes a reference, sees it's poisoned, and then immediately drops that reference and returns `MF_HUGETLB_FOLIO_PRE_POISONED`. Therefore, the *second* `madvise` call *doesn't* leak a reference. The total leaked reference is STILL exactly ONE.

So, if we have a leak of exactly one reference, then the approach in `remove_inode_hugepages` seems to be correct since we need to remove the leaked reference. But, what if `remove_inode_hugepages` *does* drop the reference but then we call `unpoison_memory`? `unpoison_memory` also *clears the poison flag AND drops a reference*. If `remove_inode_hugepages` drops it, and `unpoison_memory` *also* drops it, we have an over-release. I must now check `unpoison_memory` for hugetlb.

    "thought": "**Double Free Vulnerability in HWPoison Handling**

Okay, so I've been digging into this `unpoison_memory` function, and I think I've spotted a critical vulnerability. It seems the way we handle HWPoisoned pages, particularly in conjunction with hugepages, has a potential double-free issue.

My initial thought was that if `remove_inode_hugepages` puts a reference before clearing the HWPoison flag, there's a problem. But, I quickly realized the opposite is true. If `remove_inode_hugepages` drops the reference *before* clearing the flag, we're in trouble. `unpoison_memory` attempts to `put_page` an extra reference that has already been dropped by `remove_inode_hugepages`!

Basically, if `remove_inode_hugepages` drops that reference while *leaving* the `PG_hwpoison` flag set, then when `unpoison_memory` gets called, it sees that `PG_hwpoison` flag. It then clears it and, crucially, calls `put_page(page)` assuming there's an extra reference to drop. But *that reference is already gone*! `remove_inode_hugepages` already dropped it! Double free!

I checked the `unpoison_memory()` code to verify this:
```c
int unpoison_memory(unsigned long pfn)
{
    struct page *page;
    struct page *p;
    int freeit = 0;
...
    if (!get_hwpoison_page(p, flags)) { ... }
...
    if (TestClearPageHWPoison(p)) {
        ...
        put_page(page);
        ...
    }
}
```

Yup, that's it! `unpoison_memory` *uses* `TestClearPageHWPoison(p)` and, if it was set, it calls `put_page(page)`, assuming it must drop a leaked reference. But, consider what it's *actually* doing: `unpoison_memory` uses `put_page(page)` which is dropping the extra reference that we know comes from a `memory_failure`. If `remove_inode_hugepages` calls `folio_put(folio)` because `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` returns true... *before* `unpoison_memory` runs, then when `unpoison_memory` runs we get *another* `put_page(page)`. That's the double free. `remove_inode_hugepages` leaves the `PG_hwpoison` flag set, which triggers this behavior. I need to trace this function carefully to be certain I have all the potential paths.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of a Double Free in Hugetlb Folio Handling**

Okay, so I've been dissecting this code related to hugetlb folios and hardware memory errors, and there's a nasty concurrency bug lurking in there. It appears that a specific sequence of events leads to a double free condition, which could crash the kernel. The crux of the issue is around how we handle the "leaked" reference created when a hardware error is detected.

Here's the breakdown of what I've figured out:

1.  **The Leaked Reference:** When a hardware error occurs on a hugetlb folio, `memory_failure()` is invoked. This function, crucially, increments the folio's reference count. We can think of this as "leaking" a reference to manage the poisoned page. It also sets the `PG_hwpoison` flag. The folio *remains* in the page cache.
2.  **Unlinking/Deletion:** When the file containing the folio is unlinked or deleted, the `remove_inode_hugepages()` function is called. This function iterates over the folios and places them into an `fbatch`. At this point, the folio has a refcount of 3: 1 (pagecache) + 1 (leaked) + 1 (fbatch).
3.  **Page Cache Removal & Reference Drop:** Inside `remove_inode_hugepages()`, `remove_inode_single_folio()` is called. This function removes the folio from the page cache. At the *same* time, `remove_inode_hugepages()` is running as well. `remove_inode_hugepages` locks the folio, unmaps it, removes it from the page cache, and *then* unlocks it.

Because of the lock held by `remove_inode_hugepages`, `memory_failure`'s `folio_trylock()` will fail. *Crucially*, it correctly drops the reference it had *just* acquired, and heads for a retry loop. But, and this is where it gets interesting, it leaves the `PG_hwpoison` flag *set*. Then, `remove_inode_hugepages` (now that it has completed unlocking) checks if `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)`. Because `memory_failure` has already set the flag, `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` returns `true`. Then, `remove_inode_hugepages` *also* calls `folio_put(folio)`! That `folio_put` call drops a reference that doesn't belong to `remove_inode_hugepages` (e.g., the reference held by the `folio_batch`).

Now, fast forward: `folio_batch_release()` gets called. And guess what? It calls `folio_put()` again. This causes a *double-free* because the refcount is already underflowed. Boom, you've got a double free! It doesn't get much worse than this. This vulnerability is incredibly serious since double-frees are often a stepping-stone to privilege escalation (SLUB or buddy allocator exploitation, for example).

There's a similar race with `unpoison_memory`, too. `unpoison_memory` also drops a reference when it *clears* `PG_hwpoison`. If the removal thread dropped the reference, but left `PG_hwpoison` set, then `unpoison_memory()` will drop the refcount further, leading to the same double-free.

The fundamental flaw here is that the patch assumes that when a folio is in the pagecache and `PG_hwpoison` is true, the reference count invariant is violated, which it isn't.

So this isn't just a theoretical concern; it's a highly exploitable race condition. I am confident that I can reproduce this! The patch needs immediate attention.

    "thought": "**Double Free in Hugetlb File Handling: A Detailed Walkthrough**

Okay, so here's what I've got. I'm looking at how hugetlb folios are handled, and a potential double free bug is screaming at me.

First, `filemap_remove_folio` drops a reference count. No surprises there. Then `remove_inode_hugepages` tries to get rid of the folio. The critical part is the section dealing with hardware poison, where it does a potentially unconditional `folio_put(folio)` if `folio_test_hwpoison(folio)` is true.

The issue arises when considering the interaction with `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()`. If this function runs concurrently, it might have snagged a reference and set `PG_hwpoison`. This reference is expected to be managed by `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()`, specifically, by the call to `folio_put(folio)` within `me_huge_page()`.

Here's the problem: `me_huge_page()` checks `folio_mapping(folio)`. But, because `filemap_remove_folio` clears the mapping, `me_huge_page()` sees `mapping == NULL`. Now, *regardless* of the normal logic, it also calls `folio_put(folio)`. So we're putting a reference *twice*: once in `remove_inode_hugepages` and again in `me_huge_page()`. That's a double free, plain and simple.

I double-checked `me_huge_page()` to be absolutely certain, and yes, if `mapping == NULL`, there's an unconditional `folio_put()`. The same is true in `remove_inode_hugepages()` if `folio_test_hwpoison()` is set, which happens when a memory failure has occurred.

Now, if `memory_failure` completes before `remove_inode_hugepages`, it works: we clear the mapping, detect hwpoison, and drop the extra reference in the normal flow. But, if they run concurrently, things get nasty. `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()` grabs the refcount and sets the poison, but `remove_inode_hugepages` might run before `me_huge_page()` and see the new poison, and unconditionally drop the reference. Then, `me_huge_page()` *still* runs, and *again* calls `folio_put()`. Boom. Double free.

Wait. Are these routines serialized? `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()` does acquire `folio_lock(folio)`. And `remove_inode_single_folio()` also takes the `folio_lock`. They can't run concurrently *inside* those critical sections.

But here's the race:
1.  `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` takes `hugetlb_lock`, increments the refcount, sets the poison, then *drops* `hugetlb_lock`.
2.  Then, it tries to get `folio_lock`.
3.  Meanwhile, `remove_inode_single_folio` already holds `folio_lock`!
4.  `remove_inode_single_folio` clears the mapping, drops the `folio_lock`.
5.  Now `try_memory_failure_hugetlb` can acquire the `folio_lock`.
6.  But, after it drops the lock, we have:
    ```c
            if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
                folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
                folio_put(folio);
            }
    ```
    This `folio_put(folio)` is outside the folio lock!
7.  So, by the time it acquires the `folio_lock`, and calls `me_huge_page()`, it will call `folio_put(folio)` if the mapping is NULL.

So, the reference is dropped twice. It's an *explicit* race condition, causing the double free. My first concern, A, is completely valid and demonstrable.

Now, on to concern 2, the accounting leak. I need to look at how `free_huge_folio()` works to check that `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` doesn't leak. Let's dig in.

Okay, so I'm looking at how `remove_hugetlb_folio` works, and I see this `if (adjust_surplus) { h->surplus_huge_pages--; ... }` clause. My initial thought is, if I call `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` and then `folio_put(folio)`, I'm completely bypassing that surplus adjustment, right? That looks like a potential issue... a possible leak of the surplus counter.

But then I stop. What if this isn't a *surplus* page in the first place? What if it's a regular persistent page? For a persistent page, `adjust_surplus` should be `false` anyway! Okay, that makes more sense. Still... If it's a persistent page, how *is* it freed in the first place? I need to understand the control flow here.

Right, if `folio_test_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` is *not* set, `free_huge_folio()` checks something specific:

```c
	} else if (h->surplus_huge_pages_node[nid]) {
		/* remove the page from active list */
		remove_hugetlb_folio(h, folio, true);
		spin_unlock_irqrestore(&hugetlb_lock, flags);
		update_and_free_hugetlb_folio(h, folio, true);
	} else {
```

Ah-ha! So if the node has surplus pages, *any* freed page can be used to satisfy that surplus! Effectively, we decrement the surplus counter and return the page to the buddy allocator via `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio`. That makes perfect sense.

But if it's temporary (the case I was initially worried about), it bypasses `h->surplus_huge_pages_node[nid]`. So if there were surplus pages, they won't be adjusted, and this page WILL be freed to buddy by `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio`, but the surplus count doesn't get decremented! Okay, now I see the problem - we have a leak because we are not adjusting the surplus pages in the case that the folio is temporary.

Wait... but what if this is a persistent page that is being destroyed because of hardware poisoning?  In that case, I think that the `max_huge_pages` should be decremented.  Okay, I need to look at `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` now.

    "thought": "**Hugetlb Accounting Bug Found in Recent Patch**

Okay, so I've been digging into this patch related to hwpoisoned hugetlb pages, and I think I've found a significant problem. In `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()`, there's a crucial decrement of `h->max_huge_pages` to reflect a page being removed from the hugetlb pool. Also, `adjust_surplus` is set if there are surplus pages on the node.  This seems correct.

However, the patch *specifically* uses `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)` and `folio_put(folio)`.  This bypasses `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()` entirely by going directly to `free_huge_folio()`.  And *here's* the issue: `free_huge_folio()` *only* calls `remove_hugetlb_folio(h, folio, false)`, and crucially, *doesn't* decrement `h->max_huge_pages`! This is where the problem lies.

So, when a hwpoisoned page is dissolved through this new path, both `surplus_huge_pages` and `max_huge_pages` will be incorrect. This is a leak of hugetlb page counts!  The commit message claims this temporary marking will cleanly dissolve it, but it skips the accounting. The original `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` adjusts the accounting, but the patch circumvents it by treating it like a temporary page, which by definition never belonged to the pool to begin with. This is a very concerning problem!

If the page was a permanent page in the pagecache, removing it without decrementing `max_huge_pages` will result in a permanent leak. The system will *think* it has one more hugetlb page than is actually present.  If the page was a surplus page, then it will leak that number of surplus pages as well. This confirms the validity of concern 2 / 4 / 6 / B.

Now, I need to look into the second point regarding `madvise(MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE)` and refcount underflow, because it also seems to be a valid concern. Let me check `soft_offline_page()`.

Initially, I was thinking that the `release` flag in `page_handle_poison()` was the key. I see it's set to `!huge`, so `release` is false for hugetlb pages. This means `put_page(page)` isn't called for them in that case. But wait, `page_ref_inc(page)` *is* still called! So, if `page_handle_poison` sets `PageHWPoison(page)`, it's also increasing the reference count.

Now, looking deeper, inside `soft_offline_in_use_page()`, there's a check for `PageHWPoison(page)`. And this is where it gets interesting. If `PageHWPoison(page)` is already set *before* the isolation process, perhaps by another thread, the code unlocks the folio and, crucially, *drops* the reference obtained from `get_hwpoison_page()`. This is what's causing the problem!

    "thought": "**Analyzing Hugetlb Page Poisoning and madvise() Interaction**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was to trace the control flow starting from `__get_hwpoison_page`, which does call `get_hwpoison_hugetlb_folio`. However, I quickly saw that `get_hwpoison_hugetlb_folio` itself *doesn't* set `PG_hwpoison`. So, where is that flag actually being set in the `soft_offline_page` path?

The key seems to be within `soft_offline_in_use_page(page)`. Specifically, the call to `page_handle_poison`. That's where `SetPageHWPoison(page)` gets called, *and* `page_ref_inc(page)`. Now, I need to understand the implications of that `release` flag which, in the hugetlb case (`huge == true`), is `false`. This is important because the code path then *avoids* the `put_page(page)` in `page_handle_poison()` and *only* calls `page_ref_inc(page)`.

However, `soft_offline_in_use_page` has already put the reference from the `get_any_page()` call, so there's another reference on this folio. The code isolates the folio, migrates it, and then sets the poison flag and increments the reference. The migration is key here. I need to keep in mind the old folio is being migrated.

Then, there's `migrate_folio_done`. It conditionally releases the *source* folio, the one being migrated *from*, only if the `reason` wasn't `MR_MEMORY_FAILURE`. So, if the migration *fails*, the old folio retains the isolation reference. This means that after a migration failure, `page_handle_poison` can set `PG_hwpoison` and increment the reference *on* the old folio that now is in a failed migration state.

That old folio is then passed to `__page_handle_poison`. This calls `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio`, which seems to be the critical step. *This* function effectively removes the hugetlb folio structure from the system and frees the underlying buddy pages. It's really doing `update_and_free_hugetlb_folio`. It appears that `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` effectively frees the struct pages *after* `page_ref_inc(page)`. However, if the hugetlb page is successfully migrated, it is no longer a hugetlb page, and `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio` then frees the struct pages. Also, if `__page_handle_poison` returns > 0, the page was dissolved and taken off buddy.

Now, let's revisit concern 3/C: "If a user calls `madvise(MADV_SOFT_OFFLINE)` on a hugetlb page, `soft_offline_page()` -> `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison()` takes a reference and sets `PG_hwpoison`." This is where things get interesting, and more importantly, it's incorrect. I can see that `soft_offline_page` *never* calls `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison()`. It actually calls `get_hwpoison_page()` -> `get_any_page()` -> `__get_hwpoison_page()` -> `get_hwpoison_hugetlb_folio()`. And, as I've already established, `get_hwpoison_hugetlb_folio()` *doesn't* set `PG_hwpoison`. So, the premise of concern 3/C is factually wrong. `soft_offline_page` does *not* call `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison()`.

However, the bigger picture is still there: if `madvise(MADV_HWPOISON)` is called, then the code will call `memory_failure()`, which *does* call `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()`. I should check `madvise_inject_error`.

Okay, here's the breakdown of what I've just discovered. This is a real mess, and it centers around how the kernel handles hardware-poisoned hugetlb folios. Three distinct, severe flaws have emerged from examining the code, particularly concerning the interaction between memory failure handling, `remove_inode_hugepages`, and the `PG_hwpoison` flag. These issues can result in refcount underflows, double frees, and significant corruption of the hugetlb accounting system.

**Finding 1: The Double-Free Race Condition.**

The first major issue is a race condition between `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()` (triggered during a hardware memory error) and `remove_inode_hugepages`. Here's the scenario: `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()` sets `PG_hwpoison` and *increments the folio refcount* before *acquiring the folio lock.* Meanwhile, `remove_inode_hugepages` can run concurrently. If it wins the race and acquires the folio lock, it unmaps and removes the folio from the page cache. Then it *sees the `PG_hwpoison` flag* and calls `folio_put(folio)` to drop what it believes is the folio's final reference. When the memory-failure thread wakes up and finally acquires the folio lock, it then finds `folio_mapping(folio) == NULL`, reaches `me_huge_page()`, and, *unconditionally calls* `folio_put(folio)` *again*. This results in the same folio reference being dropped twice, leading to a refcount underflow and, ultimately, a use-after-free vulnerability. This race is completely lockless and un-synchronized.

**Finding 2: Refcount Underflow Due to `PG_hwpoison` Without an Extra Reference.**

The second critical problem arises from an incorrect assumption within the patch. It incorrectly assumes that setting `PG_hwpoison` always implies that *exactly one* extra reference is being held on the folio. This is demonstrably false. A critical example is when a hardware memory error occurs on a hugetlb folio that is temporarily isolated, such as during migration. In this case, `__get_huge_page_for_hwpoison()` *sets* `PG_hwpoison` but, crucially, *does NOT increment the refcount* (specifically, during its second pass with `MF_NO_RETRY`). `try_memory_failure_hugetlb()` then *aborts*, leaving the folio in a poisoned state but without an extra reference. Later, when `remove_inode_hugepages` processes this now un-isolated folio, it sees `PG_hwpoison` and, again, blindly calls `folio_put(folio)`. This causes a direct refcount underflow and use-after-free, because no extra reference was actually held by the memory failure subsystem.

**Finding 3: Hugetlb Accounting Corruption through Temporary Page Marking.**

The final issue I uncovered relates to how the patch handles the *permanently lost* pages due to hardware errors. By calling `folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio)`, the patch essentially *bypasses the proper hugetlb hardware poison dissolution logic*. This means that the code *avoids calling `dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio()`*, and in particular, *prevents* `max_huge_pages` from being decremented. If a surplus page is poisoned, `surplus_huge_pages` will also leak due to the bypass of `adjust_surplus`. As such, it corrupts the hugetlb accounting, causing the system to believe it has more available hugetlb pages than it actually does. The kernel's accounting becomes permanently wrong.

Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/hugetlb: fix memory offline failure due to hwpoisoned file hugetlb

When a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, me_huge_page() keeps the folio in
pagecache with an increased refcount and PG_hwpoison set. This commit aims
to fix memory offline failures by temporarily marking the folio and dropping
the memory-failure refcount when deleting it from the pagecache.

> diff --git a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> index 3f70c47981de..6bebe2e67f3e 100644
> --- a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> +++ b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> @@ -603,6 +603,11 @@ static void remove_inode_hugepages(struct inode *inode, loff_t lstart,
>  							index, truncate_op))
>  				freed++;
>  
> +			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
> +				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
> +				folio_put(folio);
> +			}
> +

Can this race with memory_failure() and cause a double free of the folio?

If try_memory_failure_hugetlb() increments the folio refcount and sets
PG_hwpoison before acquiring folio_lock():

CPU1 (memory_failure):
    __get_huge_page_for_hwpoison() // increments refcount, sets PG_hwpoison
    // blocks on folio_lock()

CPU2 (remove_inode_hugepages):
    folio_lock(folio);
    hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache(folio); // sets folio->mapping = NULL
    folio_unlock(folio);
    if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
        folio_put(folio); // drops the refcount CPU1 just acquired
    }

Then CPU1 acquires the lock, reaches me_huge_page(), sees
!folio_mapping(folio), and unconditionally calls folio_put() again,
dropping the same reference a second time.

Can this also cause a refcount underflow if PG_hwpoison is set without an
extra reference being held?

If a hardware memory error occurs on a hugetlb folio that is temporarily
isolated (like during migration), __get_huge_page_for_hwpoison() can return
MF_HUGETLB_RETRY. On its second pass with MF_NO_RETRY set, it calls
hugetlb_update_hwpoison() to set the PG_hwpoison flag but skips incrementing
the refcount. When the folio is later processed here, it will see PG_hwpoison
set and call folio_put(folio), dropping a non-existent reference.

Does bypassing dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio() here corrupt hugetlb pool
accounting?

When folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio) is called, free_huge_folio() will
eventually check this flag and call remove_hugetlb_folio(h, folio, false),
which hardcodes adjust_surplus to false. If the hwpoisoned folio was
originally a surplus page, surplus_huge_pages is never decremented.

Additionally, the normal hardware poison dissolution path in
dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio() explicitly executes h->max_huge_pages-- to
reflect the permanent loss of the page. By marking it temporary and
bypassing the dissolution function, max_huge_pages remains unmodified,
skewing the capacity statistics.

Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/hugetlb: fix memory offline failure due to hwpoisoned file hugetlb

When a file hugetlb folio triggers UCE, me_huge_page() keeps the folio in
pagecache with an increased refcount and PG_hwpoison set. This commit aims
to fix memory offline failures by temporarily marking the folio and dropping
the memory-failure refcount when deleting it from the pagecache.

> diff --git a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> index 3f70c47981de..6bebe2e67f3e 100644
> --- a/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> +++ b/fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c
> @@ -603,6 +603,11 @@ static void remove_inode_hugepages(struct inode *inode, loff_t lstart,
>  							index, truncate_op))
>  				freed++;
>  
> +			if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
> +				folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio);
> +				folio_put(folio);
> +			}
> +

Can this race with memory_failure() and cause a double free of the folio?

If try_memory_failure_hugetlb() increments the folio refcount and sets
PG_hwpoison before acquiring folio_lock():

CPU1 (memory_failure):
    __get_huge_page_for_hwpoison() // increments refcount, sets PG_hwpoison
    // blocks on folio_lock()

CPU2 (remove_inode_hugepages):
    folio_lock(folio);
    hugetlb_delete_from_page_cache(folio); // sets folio->mapping = NULL
    folio_unlock(folio);
    if (unlikely(folio_test_hwpoison(folio))) {
        folio_put(folio); // drops the refcount CPU1 just acquired
    }

Then CPU1 acquires the lock, reaches me_huge_page(), sees
!folio_mapping(folio), and unconditionally calls folio_put() again,
dropping the same reference a second time.

Can this also cause a refcount underflow if PG_hwpoison is set without an
extra reference being held?

If a hardware memory error occurs on a hugetlb folio that is temporarily
isolated (like during migration), __get_huge_page_for_hwpoison() can return
MF_HUGETLB_RETRY. On its second pass with MF_NO_RETRY set, it calls
hugetlb_update_hwpoison() to set the PG_hwpoison flag but skips incrementing
the refcount. When the folio is later processed here, it will see PG_hwpoison
set and call folio_put(folio), dropping a non-existent reference.

Does bypassing dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio() here corrupt hugetlb pool
accounting?

When folio_set_hugetlb_temporary(folio) is called, free_huge_folio() will
eventually check this flag and call remove_hugetlb_folio(h, folio, false),
which hardcodes adjust_surplus to false. If the hwpoisoned folio was
originally a surplus page, surplus_huge_pages is never decremented.

Additionally, the normal hardware poison dissolution path in
dissolve_free_hugetlb_folio() explicitly executes h->max_huge_pages-- to
reflect the permanent loss of the page. By marking it temporary and
bypassing the dissolution function, max_huge_pages remains unmodified,
skewing the capacity statistics.
